title: Sanskrit language

infobox.value: South Asia (ancient and medieval), parts of Southeast Asia (medieval), mainly Indian subcontinent
c. 1500–600 BCE (Vedic Sanskrit);[[4]](#cite_note-4)   700 BCE – 1350 CE (Classical Sanskrit)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

There are no known native speakers of Sanskrit.[[6]](#cite_note-patrick-mccartney-5-10-20-6)[[7]](#cite_note-patrick-mccartney-5-11-20-7)[[8]](#cite_note-sreevastan-thehindu-sanskrit-8)[[9]](#cite_note-Lowe2017-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Ruppel2017-10)[[11]](#cite_note-KachruKachru2008-11)

[Devanagari script](/wiki/Devanagari_script "Devanagari script") (present day). Originally orally transmitted. Not attested in writing until the 1st century BCE, when it was written in the [Brahmi script](/wiki/Brahmi_script "Brahmi script"), and later in various [Brahmic scripts](/wiki/Brahmic_scripts "Brahmic scripts").[[a]](#cite_note-Salomon1998-epigraphy-book-12)[[12]](#cite_note-JainCardona2007-script1-13)[[13]](#cite_note-JainCardona2007-script2-14)
`san` – inclusive code Individual codes: `cls` – [Classical Sanskrit](#Classical_Sanskrit) `vsn` – [Vedic Sanskrit](/wiki/Vedic_Sanskrit "Vedic Sanskrit")

infobox.key: This article contains [IPA](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Alphabet "International Phonetic Alphabet") phonetic symbols.
infobox.value: Without proper [rendering support](/wiki/Help:IPA#Rendering_issues "Help:IPA"), you may see [question marks, boxes, or other symbols](/wiki/Specials_(Unicode_block)#Replacement_character "Specials (Unicode block)") instead of [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") characters. For an introductory guide on IPA symbols, see [Help:IPA](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA"). | |

([/ˈsænskrɪt/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); stem form संस्कृत;[[15]](#cite_note-Comrie2018-cardona-luraghi-1-18)[[16]](#cite_note-wright-sanskrit-first-19) nominal singular संस्कृतम्, *saṃskṛtam*,[[17]](#cite_note-20)[[18]](#cite_note-Apte1957-59-21)[[d]](#cite_note-23)) is a [classical language](/wiki/Classical_language "Classical language") belonging to the [Indo-Aryan](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages") branch of the [Indo-European languages](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages").[[20]](#cite_note-Woodard12-24)[[21]](#cite_note-Bauer2017p90-25)[[22]](#cite_note-Ramat2015p26-26) It arose in northwestern South Asia,[[23]](#cite_note-27) deriving from Indo-Aryan languages that [diffused](/wiki/Cultural_diffusion "Cultural diffusion") from the northwest in the late [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age#South_Asia "Bronze Age").[[24]](#cite_note-Dyson2018-14-15-28)[[25]](#cite_note-TurnerSalemink2014-29) Sanskrit is the [sacred language](/wiki/Sacred_language "Sacred language") of [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") and classical [Hindu philosophy](/wiki/Hindu_philosophy "Hindu philosophy") and religion and the liturgical language of [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") and [Jainism](/wiki/Jainism "Jainism"). It was a [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") in ancient and medieval South Asia, and, as Hindu and Buddhist culture spread to Southeast East and [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia") in the [early medieval era](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages"), it became a language of religion, [high culture](/wiki/High_culture "High culture") and the political elites in some of these regions.[[26]](#cite_note-30)[[27]](#cite_note-Pollock2006-31) As a result, Sanskrit had a lasting effect on the languages of South, Southeast and parts of Eastern Asia, especially in their formal and learned vocabularies.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurrow197362–64-32)

Ancient Indo-Aryan language of South Asia, mainly Indian subcontinent

**Sanskrit** ([/ˈsænskrɪt/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); stem form संस्कृत;[[15]](#cite_note-Comrie2018-cardona-luraghi-1-18)[[16]](#cite_note-wright-sanskrit-first-19) nominal singular संस्कृतम्, *saṃskṛtam*,[[17]](#cite_note-20)[[18]](#cite_note-Apte1957-59-21)[[d]](#cite_note-23)) is a [classical language](/wiki/Classical_language "Classical language") belonging to the [Indo-Aryan](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages") branch of the [Indo-European languages](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages").[[20]](#cite_note-Woodard12-24)[[21]](#cite_note-Bauer2017p90-25)[[22]](#cite_note-Ramat2015p26-26) It arose in northwestern South Asia,[[23]](#cite_note-27) deriving from Indo-Aryan languages that [diffused](/wiki/Cultural_diffusion "Cultural diffusion") from the northwest in the late [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age#South_Asia "Bronze Age").[[24]](#cite_note-Dyson2018-14-15-28)[[25]](#cite_note-TurnerSalemink2014-29) Sanskrit is the [sacred language](/wiki/Sacred_language "Sacred language") of [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") and classical [Hindu philosophy](/wiki/Hindu_philosophy "Hindu philosophy") and religion and the liturgical language of [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") and [Jainism](/wiki/Jainism "Jainism"). It was a [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") in ancient and medieval South Asia, and, as Hindu and Buddhist culture spread to Southeast East and [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia") in the [early medieval era](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages"), it became a language of religion, [high culture](/wiki/High_culture "High culture") and the political elites in some of these regions.[[26]](#cite_note-30)[[27]](#cite_note-Pollock2006-31) As a result, Sanskrit had a lasting effect on the languages of South, Southeast and parts of Eastern Asia, especially in their formal and learned vocabularies.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurrow197362–64-32)

Sanskrit generally connotes several [Old Indo-Aryan](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages#Old_Indo-Aryan "Indo-Aryan languages") language varieties.[[29]](#cite_note-Comrie2018-cardona-luraghi-2-33)[[30]](#cite_note-Woolner1986p3-34) The most archaic of these is the [Vedic Sanskrit](/wiki/Vedic_Sanskrit "Vedic Sanskrit") found in the [Rigveda](/wiki/Rigveda "Rigveda"), a collection of 1,028 hymns composed between 1500 and 1200 BCE by Indo-Aryan tribes migrating east from the mountains of what is today northern [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") across northern [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") and into northwestern India.[[31]](#cite_note-Lowe2015-35)[[32]](#cite_note-Mair2006-RVmigration-36) Vedic Sanskrit interacted with the preexisting ancient languages of the subcontinent, absorbing names of newly encountered plants and animals; in addition, the [ancient Dravidian languages](/wiki/Proto-Dravidian_language "Proto-Dravidian language") influenced Sanskrit's [phonology](/wiki/Phonology "Phonology") and [syntax](/wiki/Syntax "Syntax").[[33]](#cite_note-Shulman2016-37) *Sanskrit* can also more narrowly refer to [Classical Sanskrit](/wiki/Classical_Sanskrit "Classical Sanskrit"), a refined and standardized grammatical form that emerged in the mid-1st millennium BCE and was codified in the most comprehensive of ancient grammars,[[e]](#cite_note-39) the *[Aṣṭādhyāyī](/wiki/A%E1%B9%A3%E1%B9%AD%C4%81dhy%C4%81y%C4%AB "Aṣṭādhyāyī")* ('Eight chapters') of [Pāṇini](/wiki/P%C4%81%E1%B9%87ini "Pāṇini").[[34]](#cite_note-Evans-2009-38) The greatest dramatist in Sanskrit, [Kālidāsa](/wiki/K%C4%81lid%C4%81sa "Kālidāsa"), wrote in classical Sanskrit, and the foundations of modern arithmetic were first described in classical Sanskrit.[[f]](#cite_note-40)[[35]](#cite_note-GlickLivesey2014-41) The two major Sanskrit epics, the [Mahābhārata](/wiki/Mah%C4%81bh%C4%81rata "Mahābhārata") and the [Rāmāyaṇa](/wiki/R%C4%81m%C4%81ya%E1%B9%87a "Rāmāyaṇa"), however, were composed in a range of oral storytelling registers called [Epic Sanskrit](/wiki/Epic_Sanskrit "Epic Sanskrit") which was used in northern India between 400 BCE and 300 CE, and roughly contemporary with classical Sanskrit.[[36]](#cite_note-Lowe2017-epic-42) In the following centuries, Sanskrit became tradition-bound, stopped being learned as a first language, and ultimately stopped developing as a living language.[[9]](#cite_note-Lowe2017-9)

The hymns of the Rigveda are notably similar to the most archaic poems of the Iranian and [Greek language](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") families, the *[Gathas](/wiki/Gatha_(Zoroaster) "Gatha (Zoroaster)")* of old [Avestan](/wiki/Avestan "Avestan") and *[Iliad](/wiki/Iliad "Iliad")* of [Homer](/wiki/Homer "Homer").[[37]](#cite_note-Lowe2015-IE-43) As the Rigveda was [orally transmitted](/wiki/Oral_tradition "Oral tradition") by [methods of memorisation](/wiki/Indian_mathematics#Styles_of_memorisation "Indian mathematics") of exceptional complexity, rigour and fidelity,[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStaal1986-44)[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFilliozat2004360–375-45) as a single text without variant readings,[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFilliozat2004139-46) its preserved archaic syntax and morphology are of vital importance in the reconstruction of the common ancestor language [Proto-Indo-European](/wiki/Proto-Indo-European "Proto-Indo-European").[[37]](#cite_note-Lowe2015-IE-43) Sanskrit does not have an attested native script: from around the turn of the 1st-millennium CE, it has been written in various [Brahmic scripts](/wiki/Brahmic_scripts "Brahmic scripts"), and in the modern era most commonly in [Devanagari](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari").[[a]](#cite_note-Salomon1998-epigraphy-book-12)[[12]](#cite_note-JainCardona2007-script1-13)[[13]](#cite_note-JainCardona2007-script2-14)

Sanskrit's status, function, and place in India's cultural heritage are recognized by its inclusion in the Constitution of India's [Eighth Schedule languages](/wiki/Languages_with_official_status_in_India#Schedule "Languages with official status in India").[[41]](#cite_note-GazzolaWickström2016-47)[[42]](#cite_note-Groff2017-lead-48) However, despite attempts at revival,[[8]](#cite_note-sreevastan-thehindu-sanskrit-8)[[43]](#cite_note-49) there are no first-language speakers of Sanskrit in India.[[8]](#cite_note-sreevastan-thehindu-sanskrit-8)[[10]](#cite_note-Ruppel2017-10)[[44]](#cite_note-KachruKachru2008-1-50) In each of India's recent decennial censuses, several thousand citizens have reported Sanskrit to be their mother tongue,[[g]](#cite_note-51) but the numbers are thought to signify a wish to be aligned with the prestige of the language.[[6]](#cite_note-patrick-mccartney-5-10-20-6)[[7]](#cite_note-patrick-mccartney-5-11-20-7)[[8]](#cite_note-sreevastan-thehindu-sanskrit-8)[[45]](#cite_note-censusofindia2011-52) Sanskrit has been taught in traditional [gurukulas](/wiki/Gurukulas "Gurukulas") since ancient times; it is widely taught today at the secondary school level. The oldest Sanskrit college is the [Benares Sanskrit College](/wiki/Benares_Sanskrit_College "Benares Sanskrit College") founded in 1791 during [East India Company rule](/wiki/Company_rule_in_India "Company rule in India").[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeth2007171–-53) Sanskrit continues to be widely used as a ceremonial and [ritual language](/wiki/Liturgical_language "Liturgical language") in Hindu and Buddhist [hymns](/wiki/Stotra "Stotra") and [chants](/wiki/Mantra "Mantra").

## Etymology and nomenclature

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Sanskrit_Manuscript_Wellcome_L0070805.jpg/250px-Sanskrit_Manuscript_Wellcome_L0070805.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sanskrit_Manuscript_Wellcome_L0070805.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Text_of_colophon_from_Sanskrit_Manuscript_on_medicine_Wellcome_L0015319.jpg/250px-Text_of_colophon_from_Sanskrit_Manuscript_on_medicine_Wellcome_L0015319.jpg)](/wiki/File:Text_of_colophon_from_Sanskrit_Manuscript_on_medicine_Wellcome_L0015319.jpg)

Historic Sanskrit manuscripts: a religious text (top), and a medical text

In Sanskrit, the [verbal adjective](/wiki/Attributive_verb#English "Attributive verb") *sáṃskṛta-* is a compound word consisting of *sáṃ* ('together, good, well, perfected') and *kṛta**-* ('made, formed, work').[[47]](#cite_note-StevensonWaite2011-54)[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShlomo_Biderman200890-55) It connotes a work that has been "well prepared, pure and perfect, polished, sacred".[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWill_Durant1963406-56)[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESir_Monier_Monier-Williams20051120-57)[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELouis_RenouJagbans_Kishore_Balbir20041–2-58) According to Biderman, the perfection contextually being referred to in the etymological origins of the word is its tonal—rather than semantic—qualities. Sound and oral transmission were highly valued qualities in ancient India, and its sages refined the alphabet, the structure of words, and its exacting grammar into a "collection of sounds, a kind of sublime musical mold" as an integral language they called *Saṃskṛta*.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShlomo_Biderman200890-55) From the late [Vedic period](/wiki/Vedic_period "Vedic period") onwards, state Annette Wilke and Oliver Moebus, resonating sound and its musical foundations attracted an "exceptionally large amount of linguistic, philosophical and religious literature" in India. Sound was visualized as "pervading all creation", another representation of the world itself; the "mysterious magnum" of Hindu thought. The search for perfection in thought and the goal of liberation were among the dimensions of sacred sound, and the common thread that wove all ideas and inspirations together became the quest for what the ancient Indians believed to be a perfect language, the "phonocentric episteme" of Sanskrit.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnnette_WilkeOliver_Moebus201162–66_with_footnotes-59)[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuy_L._Beck2006117–123-60)

Sanskrit as a language competed with numerous, less exact vernacular Indian languages called *Prakritic languages* (*[prākṛta](/wiki/Prakrit "Prakrit")-*). The term *prakṛta* literally means "original, natural, normal, artless", states [Franklin Southworth](/wiki/Franklin_Southworth "Franklin Southworth").[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESouthworth200445-61) The relationship between Prakrit and Sanskrit is found in Indian texts dated to the 1st millennium CE. [Patañjali](/wiki/Pata%C3%B1jali "Patañjali") acknowledged that Prakrit is the first language, one instinctively adopted by every child with all its imperfections and later leads to the problems of interpretation and misunderstanding. The purifying structure of the Sanskrit language removes these imperfections. The early Sanskrit grammarian [Daṇḍin](/wiki/Da%E1%B9%87%E1%B8%8Din "Daṇḍin") states, for example, that much in the Prakrit languages is etymologically rooted in Sanskrit, but involves "loss of sounds" and corruptions that result from a "disregard of the grammar". Daṇḍin acknowledged that there are words and confusing structures in Prakrit that thrive independent of Sanskrit. This view is found in the writing of [Bharata Muni](/wiki/Bharata_Muni "Bharata Muni"), the author of the ancient *[Natya Shastra](/wiki/Natya_Shastra "Natya Shastra")* text. The early [Jain](/wiki/Jain "Jain") scholar Namisādhu acknowledged the difference, but disagreed that Prakrit language was a corruption of Sanskrit. Namisādhu stated that the Prakrit language was the *pūrvam* ('came before, origin') and that it came naturally to children, while Sanskrit was a refinement of Prakrit through "purification by grammar".[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKleinJosephFritz2017318–320-62)

## History

### Origin and development

See also: [Indo-European vocabulary](/wiki/Indo-European_vocabulary "Indo-European vocabulary"), [Proto-Indo-Aryan language](/wiki/Proto-Indo-Aryan_language "Proto-Indo-Aryan language"), and [Indo-Iranian languages](/wiki/Indo-Iranian_languages "Indo-Iranian languages")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/IE_expansion.png/250px-IE_expansion.png)](/wiki/File:IE_expansion.png)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/IE1500BP.png/250px-IE1500BP.png)](/wiki/File:IE1500BP.png)

Left: The [Kurgan hypothesis](/wiki/Kurgan_hypothesis "Kurgan hypothesis") on [Indo-European migrations](/wiki/Indo-European_migrations "Indo-European migrations") between 4000 and 1000 BCE; right: The geographical spread of the Indo-European languages at 500 CE, with Sanskrit in South Asia

Sanskrit belongs to the [Indo-European family of languages](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages"). It is one of the three earliest ancient documented languages that arose from a common root language now referred to as [Proto-Indo-European](/wiki/Proto-Indo-European_language "Proto-Indo-European language"):[[20]](#cite_note-Woodard12-24)[[21]](#cite_note-Bauer2017p90-25)[[22]](#cite_note-Ramat2015p26-26)

* [Vedic Sanskrit](/wiki/Vedic_Sanskrit "Vedic Sanskrit") (c. 1500–500 BCE).
* [Mycenaean Greek](/wiki/Mycenaean_Greek "Mycenaean Greek") (c. 1450 BCE)[[56]](#cite_note-63)
* [Hittite](/wiki/Hittite_language "Hittite language") (c. 1750–1200 BCE).

Other Indo-European languages distantly related to Sanskrit include [archaic](/wiki/Old_Latin "Old Latin") and [Classical Latin](/wiki/Classical_Latin "Classical Latin") (c. 600 BCE–100 CE, [Italic languages](/wiki/Italic_languages "Italic languages")), [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_language "Gothic language") (archaic [Germanic language](/wiki/Germanic_language "Germanic language"), c. 350 CE), [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse "Old Norse") (c. 200 CE and after), [Old Avestan](/wiki/Old_Avestan "Old Avestan") (c. late 2nd millennium BCE[[57]](#cite_note-64)) and [Younger Avestan](/wiki/Avestan_language "Avestan language") (c. 900 BCE).[[21]](#cite_note-Bauer2017p90-25)[[22]](#cite_note-Ramat2015p26-26) The closest ancient relatives of Vedic Sanskrit in the Indo-European languages are the [Nuristani languages](/wiki/Nuristani_language "Nuristani language") found in the remote [Hindu Kush](/wiki/Hindu_Kush "Hindu Kush") region of northeastern Afghanistan and northwestern [Himalayas](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas"),[[22]](#cite_note-Ramat2015p26-26)[[58]](#cite_note-DaniMasson1999-65)[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasica199334-66) as well as the extinct [Avestan](/wiki/Avestan "Avestan") and [Old Persian](/wiki/Old_Persian "Old Persian") – both [Iranian languages](/wiki/Iranian_languages "Iranian languages").[[60]](#cite_note-67)[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBryantPatton2005208-68)[[62]](#cite_note-Robins2014p346-69) Sanskrit belongs to the [satem](/wiki/Centum_and_satem_languages "Centum and satem languages") group of the Indo-European languages.

Colonial era scholars familiar with Latin and Greek were struck by the resemblance of the Sanskrit language, both in its vocabulary and grammar, to the classical languages of Europe.
In *The Oxford Introduction to Proto-Indo-European and the Proto-Indo-European World*, Mallory and Adams illustrate the resemblance with the following examples of [cognate](/wiki/Cognate "Cognate") forms[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJ._P._MalloryD._Q._Adams20066-70) (with the addition of Old English for further comparison):

| PIE | English | Old English | Latin | Ancient Greek | **Sanskrit** | Glossary |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| \*méh₂tēr | mother | mōdor | māter | mētēr | **mātṛ́-** | mother |
| \*ph₂tḗr | father | fæder | pater | patēr | **pitṛ́-** | father |
| \*bʰréh₂tēr | brother | brōþor | frāter | phreter | **bhrā́tṛ-** | brother |
| \*swésōr | sister | sweoster | soror | eor | **svásṛ-** | sister |
| \*suHnús | son | sunu | - | hyiós | **sūnú-** | son |
| \*dʰugh₂tḗr | daughter | dohtor | - | thugátēr | **duhitṛ́-** | daughter |
| \*gʷṓws | cow | cū | bōs | bous | **gáu-** | cow |
| \*demh₂- | tame, timber | tam, timber | domus | dom- | **dām-** | house, tame, build |

The correspondences suggest some common root, and historical links between some of the distant major ancient languages of the world.[[h]](#cite_note-72)

The [Indo-Aryan migrations](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_migrations "Indo-Aryan migrations") theory explains the common features shared by Sanskrit and other Indo-European languages by proposing that the original speakers of what became Sanskrit arrived in South Asia from a region of common origin, somewhere north-west of the [Indus region](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River"), during the early 2nd millennium BCE. Evidence for such a theory includes the close relationship between the Indo-Iranian tongues and the [Baltic](/wiki/Baltic_languages "Baltic languages") and [Slavic languages](/wiki/Slavic_languages "Slavic languages"), vocabulary exchange with the non-Indo-European [Uralic languages](/wiki/Uralic_languages "Uralic languages"), and the nature of the attested Indo-European words for flora and fauna.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasica199336–38-73)

The pre-history of Indo-Aryan languages which preceded Vedic Sanskrit is unclear and various hypotheses place it over a fairly wide limit. According to Thomas Burrow, based on the relationship between various Indo-European languages, the origin of all these languages may possibly be in what is now Central or Eastern Europe, while the Indo-Iranian group possibly arose in Central Russia.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurrow197330–32-74) The Iranian and Indo-Aryan branches separated quite early. It is the Indo-Aryan branch that moved into eastern Iran and then south into South Asia in the first half of the 2nd millennium BCE. Once in ancient India, the Indo-Aryan language underwent rapid linguistic change and morphed into the Vedic Sanskrit language.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurrow197330–34-75)

### Vedic Sanskrit

Main article: [Vedic Sanskrit](/wiki/Vedic_Sanskrit "Vedic Sanskrit")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Rigveda_MS2097.jpg/500px-Rigveda_MS2097.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rigveda_MS2097.jpg)

*Rigveda* ([padapatha](/wiki/Vedic_chant "Vedic chant")) manuscript in [Devanagari](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari"), early 19th century. The red horizontal and vertical lines mark low and high pitch changes for chanting.

The pre-Classical form of Sanskrit is known as *[Vedic Sanskrit](/wiki/Vedic_Sanskrit "Vedic Sanskrit")*. The earliest attested Sanskrit text is the [Rigveda](/wiki/Rigveda "Rigveda") (*Ṛg-veda*), a Hindu scripture from the mid- to late-second millennium BCE. No written records from such an early period survive, if any ever existed, but scholars are generally confident that the oral transmission of the texts is reliable: they are ceremonial literature, where the exact phonetic expression and its preservation were a part of the historic tradition.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeier-Brügger200320-76)[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacDonell2004-77)[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeith19963–4-78)

However some scholars have suggested that the original Ṛg-veda differed in some fundamental ways in phonology compared to the sole surviving version available to us. In particular that retroflex consonants did not exist as a natural part of the earliest Vedic language,[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeshpande1993165-79) and that these developed in the centuries after the composition had been completed, and as a gradual unconscious process during the oral transmission by generations of reciters.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The primary source for this argument is internal evidence of the text which betrays an instability of the phenomenon of retroflexion, with the same phrases having sandhi-induced retroflexion in some parts but not other.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBloomfieldEdgerton1932§163–170-80) This is taken along with evidence of controversy, for example, in passages of the Aitareya-Āraṇyaka (700 BCE), which features a discussion on whether retroflexion is valid in particular cases.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeshpande1993130–196-81)

The Ṛg-veda is a collection of books, created by multiple authors. These authors represented different generations, and the mandalas 2 to 7 are the oldest while the mandalas 1 and 10 are relatively the youngest.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarbara_A._Holdrege2012229–230-82)[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBryant200166–67-83) Yet, the Vedic Sanskrit in these books of the Ṛg-veda "hardly presents any dialectical diversity", states [Louis Renou](/wiki/Louis_Renou "Louis Renou") – an [Indologist](/wiki/Indology "Indology") known for his scholarship of the Sanskrit literature and the Ṛg-veda in particular. According to Renou, this implies that the Vedic Sanskrit language had a "set linguistic pattern" by the second half of the 2nd millennium BCE.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELouis_RenouJagbans_Kishore_Balbir20045–6-84) Beyond the Ṛg-veda, the ancient literature in Vedic Sanskrit that has survived into the modern age include the *Samaveda*, *Yajurveda*, *Atharvaveda*, along with the embedded and layered Vedic texts such as the [Brahmanas](/wiki/Brahmana "Brahmana"), [Aranyakas](/wiki/Aranyaka "Aranyaka"), and the early [Upanishads](/wiki/Upanishad "Upanishad").[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeier-Brügger200320-76) These Vedic documents reflect the dialects of Sanskrit found in the various parts of the northwestern, northern, and eastern Indian subcontinent.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECardona2012-85)[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWitzel19979-86)

According to Michael Witzel, Vedic Sanskrit was a spoken language of the semi-nomadic [Aryans](/wiki/Aryan "Aryan").[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWitzel199716–17-87)[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarold_G._Coward19903–12,_36–47,_111–112,_Note:_Sanskrit_was_both_a_literary_and_spoken_language_in_ancient_India.-88) The Vedic Sanskrit language or a closely related Indo-European variant was recognized beyond ancient India as evidenced by the "[Mitanni](/wiki/Mitanni "Mitanni") Treaty" between the ancient Hittite and [Mitanni](/wiki/Mitanni "Mitanni") people, carved into a rock, in a region that now includes parts of [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria") and [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey").[[81]](#cite_note-Cohen2017p11-89)[[i]](#cite_note-91) Parts of this treaty, such as the names of the Mitanni princes and technical terms related to horse training, for reasons not understood, are in early forms of Vedic Sanskrit. The treaty also invokes the gods [Varuna](/wiki/Varuna "Varuna"), [Mitra](/wiki/Mitra "Mitra"), [Indra](/wiki/Indra "Indra"), and [Nasatya](/wiki/Ashvins "Ashvins") found in the earliest layers of the Vedic literature.[[81]](#cite_note-Cohen2017p11-89)[[83]](#cite_note-92)

> O Bṛhaspati, when in giving names  
> they first set forth the beginning of Language,  
> Their most excellent and spotless secret  
> was laid bare through love,  
> When the wise ones formed Language with their mind,  
> purifying it like grain with a winnowing fan,  
> Then friends knew friendships –  
> an auspicious mark placed on their language.

— *[Rigveda](/wiki/Rigveda "Rigveda") 10.71.1–4*  
Translated by Roger Woodard[[84]](#cite_note-93)

The Vedic Sanskrit found in the Ṛg-veda is distinctly more archaic than other Vedic texts, and in many respects, the Rigvedic language is notably more similar to those found in the archaic texts of Old Avestan [Zoroastrian](/wiki/Zoroastrianism "Zoroastrianism") *Gathas* and Homer's *Iliad* and *Odyssey*.[[85]](#cite_note-Lowe2015p2-94) According to Stephanie W. Jamison and Joel P. Brereton – Indologists known for their translation of the Ṛg-veda – the Vedic Sanskrit literature "clearly inherited" from Indo-Iranian and Indo-European times the social structures such as the role of the poet and the priests, the patronage economy, the phrasal equations, and some of the poetic metres.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStephanie_W._JamisonJoel_P._Brereton201410–11,_72-95)[[j]](#cite_note-97) While there are similarities, state Jamison and Brereton, there are also differences between Vedic Sanskrit, the Old Avestan, and the Mycenaean Greek literature. For example, [simile](/wiki/Simile "Simile") is deployed extensively in the Sanskrit Ṛg-veda, whereas the Old Avestan *Gathas* lack simile entirely (and it is rare in later forms of the language); conversely, simile is common in [Homeric Greek](/wiki/Homeric_Greek "Homeric Greek"), but they are structurally very different to those in Ṛg-vedic Sanskrit.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStephanie_W._JamisonJoel_P._Brereton201466–67-98)

### Classical Sanskrit

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Birch_bark_MS_from_Kashmir_of_the_Rupavatra_Wellcome_L0032691.jpg/330px-Birch_bark_MS_from_Kashmir_of_the_Rupavatra_Wellcome_L0032691.jpg)](/wiki/File:Birch_bark_MS_from_Kashmir_of_the_Rupavatra_Wellcome_L0032691.jpg)

A 17th-century birch bark manuscript of Pāṇini's grammar treatise from Kashmir

The early Vedic form of the Sanskrit language was far less homogenous compared to the Classical Sanskrit as defined by grammarians by about the mid-1st millennium BCE. According to Richard Gombrich—an Indologist and a scholar of Sanskrit, Pāli and Buddhist Studies—the archaic Vedic Sanskrit found in the *Rigveda* had already evolved in the Vedic period, as evidenced in the later Vedic literature. Gombrich posits that the language in the early Upanishads of Hinduism and the late Vedic literature approaches Classical Sanskrit, while the archaic Vedic Sanskrit had by the [Buddha](/wiki/Buddha "Buddha")'s time become unintelligible to all except ancient Indian sages.[[89]](#cite_note-Gombrich2006p24-99)

The formalization of the Saṃskṛta language is credited to *[Pāṇini](/wiki/P%C4%81%E1%B9%87ini "Pāṇini")*, along with Patañjali's *Mahābhāṣya* and Katyayana's commentary that preceded Patañjali's work.[[90]](#cite_note-100) Panini composed *Aṣṭādhyāyī* ('Eight-Chapter Grammar'), which became the foundation of Vyākaraṇa, a [Vedānga](/wiki/Vedanga "Vedanga").[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarold_G._Coward199013–14,_111-101) The *Aṣṭādhyāyī* was not the first description of Sanskrit grammar, but it is the earliest that has survived in full, and the culmination of a long grammatical tradition that Fortson says, is "one of the intellectual wonders of the ancient world".[[92]](#cite_note-Fortson,_§10.26-102) Pāṇini cites ten scholars on the phonological and grammatical aspects of the Sanskrit language before him, as well as the variants in the usage of Sanskrit in different regions of India.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarold_G._Coward199013–14,_111-101) The ten Vedic scholars he quotes are Āpiśali, [Kaśyapa](/wiki/Kashyapa "Kashyapa"), Gārgya, Gālava, Cakravarmaṇa, [Bhāradvāja](/wiki/Bharadvaja "Bharadvaja"), Śākaṭāyana, Śākalya, Senaka and Sphoṭāyana.[[93]](#cite_note-103)[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECardona19972-104)

In the *Aṣṭādhyāyī*, language is observed in a manner that has no parallel among Greek or Latin grammarians. Pāṇini's grammar, according to Renou and Filliozat, is a classic that defines the linguistic expression and sets the standard for the Sanskrit language.[[95]](#cite_note-105) Pāṇini made use of a technical metalanguage consisting of a syntax, morphology and lexicon. This metalanguage is organised according to a series of meta-rules, some of which are explicitly stated while others can be deduced.[[96]](#cite_note-106) Despite differences in the analysis from that of modern linguistics, Pāṇini's work has been found valuable and the most advanced analysis of linguistics until the twentieth century.[[92]](#cite_note-Fortson,_§10.26-102)

Pāṇini's comprehensive and scientific theory of grammar is conventionally taken to mark the start of Classical Sanskrit.[[97]](#cite_note-107) His systematic treatise inspired and made Sanskrit the preeminent Indian language of learning and literature for two millennia.[[98]](#cite_note-bowman328-108) It is unclear whether Pāṇini himself wrote his treatise or he orally created the detailed and sophisticated treatise then transmitted it through his students. Modern scholarship generally accepts that he knew of a form of writing, based on references to words such as *[lipi](/wiki/Lipi_(script) "Lipi (script)")* ('script') and *lipikara* ('scribe') in section 3.2 of the *Aṣṭādhyāyī*.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon199811-109)[[100]](#cite_note-Juhyung_Rhi_2009_5,_1–13-110)[[101]](#cite_note-111)[[k]](#cite_note-118)

The Classical Sanskrit language formalized by Pāṇini, states Renou, is "not an impoverished language", rather it is "a controlled and a restrained language from which archaisms and unnecessary formal alternatives were excluded".[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELouis_RenouJagbans_Kishore_Balbir200453-119) The Classical form of the language simplified the *sandhi* rules but retained various aspects of the Vedic language, while adding rigor and flexibilities, so that it had sufficient means to express thoughts as well as being "capable of responding to the future increasing demands of an infinitely diversified literature", according to Renou. Pāṇini included numerous "optional rules" beyond the Vedic Sanskrit's *bahulam* framework, to respect liberty and creativity so that individual writers separated by geography or time would have the choice to express facts and their views in their own way, where tradition followed competitive forms of the Sanskrit language.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELouis_RenouJagbans_Kishore_Balbir200453–54-120)

The phonetic differences between Vedic Sanskrit and Classical Sanskrit, as discerned from the current state of the surviving literature,[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeshpande1993130–196-81) are negligible when compared to the intense change that must have occurred in the pre-Vedic period between the Proto-Indo-Aryan language and Vedic Sanskrit.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurrow197333–34-121) The noticeable differences between the Vedic and the Classical Sanskrit include the much-expanded grammar and grammatical categories as well as the differences in the accent, the semantics and the syntax.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEA._M._Ruppel2017378–383-122) There are also some differences between how some of the nouns and verbs end, as well as the *sandhi* rules, both internal and external.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEA._M._Ruppel2017378–383-122) Quite many words found in the early Vedic Sanskrit language are never found in late Vedic Sanskrit or Classical Sanskrit literature, while some words have different and new meanings in Classical Sanskrit when contextually compared to the early Vedic Sanskrit literature.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEA._M._Ruppel2017378–383-122)

[Arthur Macdonell](/wiki/Arthur_Macdonell "Arthur Macdonell") was among the early colonial era scholars who summarized some of the differences between the Vedic and Classical Sanskrit.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEA._M._Ruppel2017378–383-122)[[112]](#cite_note-123) Louis Renou published in 1956, in French, a more extensive discussion of the similarities, the differences and the evolution of the Vedic Sanskrit within the Vedic period and then to the Classical Sanskrit along with his views on the history. This work has been translated by Jagbans Balbir.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELouis_RenouJagbans_Kishore_Balbir20041–59-124)

### Sanskrit and Prakrit languages

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Word_for_Sanskrit_Samskrita_in_the_Mandsaur_stone_inscription_of_Yashodharman-Vishnuvardhana_532_CE.jpg/250px-Word_for_Sanskrit_Samskrita_in_the_Mandsaur_stone_inscription_of_Yashodharman-Vishnuvardhana_532_CE.jpg)](/wiki/File:Word_for_Sanskrit_Samskrita_in_the_Mandsaur_stone_inscription_of_Yashodharman-Vishnuvardhana_532_CE.jpg)

An early use of the word for "Sanskrit" in Late [Brahmi script](/wiki/Brahmi_script "Brahmi script") (also called [Gupta script](/wiki/Gupta_script "Gupta script")):

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Gupta_ashoka_sam.jpg/20px-Gupta_ashoka_sam.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gupta_ashoka_sam.jpg)[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Gupta_ashoka_skrr.jpg/20px-Gupta_ashoka_skrr.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gupta_ashoka_skrr.jpg)[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Gupta_ashoka_t.svg/20px-Gupta_ashoka_t.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Gupta_ashoka_t.svg) *Saṃ-skṛ-ta*

  
[Mandsaur stone inscription of Yashodharman-Vishnuvardhana](/wiki/Mandsaur_stone_inscription_of_Yashodharman-Vishnuvardhana "Mandsaur stone inscription of Yashodharman-Vishnuvardhana"), 532 CE.[[114]](#cite_note-JFF-125)

The earliest known use of the word *Saṃskṛta* (Sanskrit), in the context of a speech or language, is found in verses 5.28.17–19 of the *Ramayana*.[[16]](#cite_note-wright-sanskrit-first-19) Outside the learned sphere of written Classical Sanskrit, vernacular colloquial dialects ([Prakrits](/wiki/Prakrit "Prakrit")) continued to evolve. Sanskrit co-existed with numerous other Prakrit languages of ancient India. The Prakrit languages of India also have ancient roots and some Sanskrit scholars have called these *Apabhramsa*, literally 'spoiled'.[[115]](#cite_note-126)[[116]](#cite_note-127) The Vedic literature includes words whose phonetic equivalent are not found in other [Indo-European languages](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages") but which are found in the regional Prakrit languages, which makes it likely that the interaction, the sharing of words and ideas began early in the Indian history. As the Indian thought diversified and challenged earlier beliefs of Hinduism, particularly in the form of [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") and [Jainism](/wiki/Jainism "Jainism"), the Prakrit languages such as [Pali](/wiki/Pali "Pali") in [Theravada](/wiki/Theravada "Theravada") Buddhism and Ardhamagadhi in Jainism competed with Sanskrit in the ancient times.[[117]](#cite_note-128)[[118]](#cite_note-129)[[119]](#cite_note-dundas69-130) However, states [Paul Dundas](/wiki/Paul_Dundas "Paul Dundas"), these ancient Prakrit languages had "roughly the same relationship to Sanskrit as medieval Italian does to Latin".[[119]](#cite_note-dundas69-130) The Indian tradition states that the [Buddha](/wiki/Buddha "Buddha") and the [Mahavira](/wiki/Mahavira "Mahavira") preferred the Prakrit language so that everyone could understand it. However, scholars such as Dundas have questioned this hypothesis. They state that there is no evidence for this and whatever evidence is available suggests that by the start of the common era, hardly anybody other than learned monks had the capacity to understand the old Prakrit languages such as [Ardhamagadhi](/wiki/Ardhamagadhi "Ardhamagadhi").[[119]](#cite_note-dundas69-130)

A section of European scholars state that Sanskrit was never a spoken language.[[120]](#cite_note-Shastri1987p20-131) However, evidences shows that Sanskrit was a spoken language, essential for [oral tradition](/wiki/Oral_tradition "Oral tradition") that preserved the vast number of Sanskrit manuscripts from ancient India. The textual evidence in the works of Yaksa, Panini, and Patanajali affirms that Classical Sanskrit in their era was a spoken language (*bhasha*) used by the cultured and educated. Some *[sutras](/wiki/Sutra "Sutra")* expound upon the variant forms of spoken Sanskrit versus written Sanskrit.[[120]](#cite_note-Shastri1987p20-131) Chinese Buddhist pilgrim [Xuanzang](/wiki/Xuanzang "Xuanzang") mentioned in his memoir that official philosophical debates in India were held in Sanskrit, not in the vernacular language of that region.[[120]](#cite_note-Shastri1987p20-131)

[![Tree diagram showing genetic relationships among languages](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/IndoEuropeanTree.svg/960px-IndoEuropeanTree.svg.png)](/wiki/File:IndoEuropeanTree.svg "Sanskrit's link to the Prakrit languages and other Indo-European languages")

Sanskrit's link to the Prakrit languages and other Indo-European languages

According to Sanskrit linguist professor Madhav Deshpande, Sanskrit was a spoken language in a colloquial form by the mid-1st millennium BCE which coexisted with a more formal, grammatically correct form of literary Sanskrit.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeshpande2011218–220-132) This, states Deshpande, is true for modern languages where colloquial incorrect approximations and dialects of a language are spoken and understood, along with more "refined, sophisticated and grammatically accurate" forms of the same language being found in the literary works.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeshpande2011218–220-132) The Indian tradition, states [Winternitz](/wiki/Moriz_Winternitz "Moriz Winternitz"), has favored the learning and the usage of multiple languages from the ancient times. Sanskrit was a spoken language in the educated and the elite classes, but it was also a language that must have been understood in a wider circle of society because the widely popular folk epics and stories such as the *[Ramayana](/wiki/Ramayana "Ramayana")*, the *[Mahabharata](/wiki/Mahabharata "Mahabharata")*, the *[Bhagavata Purana](/wiki/Bhagavata_Purana "Bhagavata Purana")*, the *[Panchatantra](/wiki/Panchatantra "Panchatantra")* and many other texts are all in the Sanskrit language.[[122]](#cite_note-133) The Classical Sanskrit with its exacting grammar was thus the language of the Indian scholars and the educated classes, while others communicated with approximate or ungrammatical variants of it as well as other natural Indian languages.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeshpande2011218–220-132) Sanskrit, as the learned language of Ancient India, thus existed alongside the vernacular Prakrits.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeshpande2011218–220-132) Many [Sanskrit dramas](/wiki/Sanskrit_drama "Sanskrit drama") indicate that the language coexisted with the vernacular Prakrits. The cities of [Varanasi](/wiki/Varanasi "Varanasi"), [Paithan](/wiki/Paithan "Paithan"), [Pune](/wiki/Pune "Pune") and [Kanchipuram](/wiki/Kanchipuram "Kanchipuram") were centers of classical Sanskrit learning and public debates until the arrival of the colonial era.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeshpande2011222–223-134)

According to [Lamotte](/wiki/%C3%89tienne_Lamotte "Étienne Lamotte"), Sanskrit became the dominant literary and inscriptional language because of its precision in communication. It was, states Lamotte, an ideal instrument for presenting ideas, and as knowledge in Sanskrit multiplied, so did its spread and influence.[[124]](#cite_note-135) Sanskrit was adopted voluntarily as a vehicle of high culture, arts, and profound ideas. Pollock disagrees with Lamotte, but concurs that Sanskrit's influence grew into what he terms a "Sanskrit Cosmopolis" over a region that included all of South Asia and much of southeast Asia. The Sanskrit language cosmopolis thrived beyond India between 300 and 1300 CE.[[125]](#cite_note-pollock1996p197-136)

Today, it is believed that Kashmiri is the closest language to Sanskrit.[[126]](#cite_note-137)[[127]](#cite_note-138)[[128]](#cite_note-139)

### Dravidian influence on Sanskrit

See also: [Dravidian languages § Dravidian influence on Sanskrit](/wiki/Dravidian_languages#Dravidian_influence_on_Sanskrit "Dravidian languages")

Reinöhl mentions that not only have the Dravidian languages borrowed from Sanskrit vocabulary, but they have also affected Sanskrit on deeper levels of structure, "for instance in the domain of phonology where Indo-Aryan retroflexes have been attributed to Dravidian influence".[[129]](#cite_note-:02-140) Similarly, Ferenc Ruzca states that all the major shifts in Indo-Aryan phonetics over two millennia can be attributed to the constant influence of a Dravidian language with a similar phonetic structure to Tamil.[[130]](#cite_note-141) Hock et al. quoting George Hart state that there was influence of [Old Tamil](/wiki/Old_Tamil "Old Tamil") on Sanskrit.[[131]](#cite_note-:11-142) Hart compared Old Tamil and Classical Sanskrit to arrive at a conclusion that there was a common language from which these features both derived – "that both Tamil and Sanskrit derived their shared conventions, metres, and techniques from a common source, for it is clear that neither borrowed directly from the other."[[132]](#cite_note-143)

Reinöhl further states that there is a symmetric relationship between Dravidian languages like Kannada or Tamil, with Indo-Aryan languages like Bengali or Hindi, whereas the same relationship is not found for non-Indo-Aryan languages, for example, Persian or English:

> A sentence in a Dravidian language like Tamil or Kannada becomes ordinarily good Bengali or Hindi by substituting Bengali or Hindi equivalents for the Dravidian words and forms, without modifying the word order; but the same thing is not possible in rendering a Persian or English sentence into a non-Indo-Aryan language.

— Reinöhl[[129]](#cite_note-:02-140)

Shulman mentions that "Dravidian nonfinite verbal forms (called *vinaiyeccam* in Tamil) shaped the usage of the Sanskrit nonfinite verbs (originally derived from inflected forms of action nouns in Vedic). This particularly salient case of the possible influence of Dravidian on Sanskrit is only one of many items of syntactic assimilation, not least among them the large repertoire of morphological modality and aspect that, once one knows to look for it, can be found everywhere in classical and postclassical Sanskrit".[[133]](#cite_note-:8-144)

The main influence of Dravidian on Sanskrit is found to have been concentrated in the timespan between the late Vedic period and the crystallization of Classical Sanskrit. As in this period the [Indo-Aryan](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_peoples "Indo-Aryan peoples") tribes had not yet made contact with the inhabitants of the South of the subcontinent, this suggests a significant presence of Dravidian speakers in North India (the central Gangetic plain and the classical Madhyadeśa) who were instrumental in this substratal influence on Sanskrit.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurrow1973386-145)

## Influence

> Extant manuscripts in Sanskrit number over 30 million, one hundred times those in Greek and Latin combined, constituting the largest cultural heritage that any civilization has produced prior to the invention of the printing press.

— Foreword of *Sanskrit Computational Linguistics* (2009), Gérard Huet, Amba Kulkarni and Peter Scharf[[135]](#cite_note-146)[[136]](#cite_note-147)[[l]](#cite_note-149)

Sanskrit has been the predominant language of [Hindu texts](/wiki/Hindu_texts "Hindu texts") encompassing a rich tradition of [philosophical](/wiki/Hindu_philosophy "Hindu philosophy") and [religious](/wiki/Dharma "Dharma") texts, as well as poetry, music, [drama](/wiki/Sanskrit_drama "Sanskrit drama"), [scientific](/wiki/Scientific_literature "Scientific literature"), technical and others.[[138]](#cite_note-150)[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBanerji1989618–632,_see_also_the_extended_list_of_Sanskrit_texts_in_Part_II-151) It is the predominant language of one of the largest collection of historic manuscripts. The earliest known inscriptions in Sanskrit are from the 1st century BCE, such as the [Ayodhya Inscription of Dhana](/wiki/Ayodhya_Inscription_of_Dhana "Ayodhya Inscription of Dhana") and [Ghosundi-Hathibada (Chittorgarh)](/wiki/Hathibada_Ghosundi_inscriptions "Hathibada Ghosundi inscriptions").[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon199886–87-152)

Though developed and nurtured by scholars of orthodox schools of Hinduism, Sanskrit has been the language for some of the key literary works and theology of heterodox schools of Indian philosophies such as Buddhism and Jainism.[[141]](#cite_note-Parret1976p102-153)[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurrow197357–64,_289,_319-154) The structure and capabilities of the Classical Sanskrit language launched ancient Indian speculations about "the nature and function of language", what is the relationship between words and their meanings in the context of a community of speakers, whether this relationship is objective or subjective, discovered or is created, how individuals learn and relate to the world around them through language, and about the limits of language.[[141]](#cite_note-Parret1976p102-153)[[143]](#cite_note-deshpande2010-155) They speculated on the role of language, the ontological status of painting word-images through sound, and the need for rules so that it can serve as a means for a community of speakers, separated by geography or time, to share and understand profound ideas from each other.[[143]](#cite_note-deshpande2010-155)[[m]](#cite_note-158) These speculations became particularly important to the [Mīmāṃsā](/wiki/M%C4%ABm%C4%81%E1%B9%83s%C4%81 "Mīmāṃsā") and the [Nyaya](/wiki/Nyaya "Nyaya") schools of Hindu philosophy, and later to Vedanta and Mahayana Buddhism, states [Frits Staal](/wiki/Frits_Staal "Frits Staal")—a scholar of Linguistics with a focus on Indian philosophies and Sanskrit.[[141]](#cite_note-Parret1976p102-153) Though written in a number of different scripts, the dominant language of Hindu texts has been Sanskrit. It or a hybrid form of Sanskrit became the preferred language of Mahayana Buddhism scholarship;[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWayman1965111–115-159) for example, one of the early and influential Buddhist philosophers, [Nagarjuna](/wiki/Nagarjuna "Nagarjuna") (c.200 CE), used Classical Sanskrit as the language for his texts.[[147]](#cite_note-160) According to Renou, Sanskrit had a limited role in the Theravada tradition (formerly known as the Hinayana) but the Prakrit works that have survived are of doubtful authenticity. Some of the canonical fragments of the early Buddhist traditions, discovered in the 20th century, suggest the early Buddhist traditions used an imperfect and reasonably good Sanskrit, sometimes with a Pali syntax, states Renou. The [Mahāsāṃghika](/wiki/Mah%C4%81s%C4%81%E1%B9%83ghika "Mahāsāṃghika") and Mahavastu, in their late Hinayana forms, used hybrid Sanskrit for their literature.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELouis_RenouJagbans_Kishore_Balbir2004177–180-161) Sanskrit was also the language of some of the oldest surviving, authoritative and much followed philosophical works of Jainism such as the *Tattvartha Sutra* by [Umaswati](/wiki/Umaswati "Umaswati").[[n]](#cite_note-163)[[150]](#cite_note-164)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/2nd-century_CE_Sanskrit%2C_Kizil_China%2C_Spitzer_Manuscript_folio_383_fragment_recto_and_verso.jpg/330px-2nd-century_CE_Sanskrit%2C_Kizil_China%2C_Spitzer_Manuscript_folio_383_fragment_recto_and_verso.jpg)](/wiki/File:2nd-century_CE_Sanskrit,_Kizil_China,_Spitzer_Manuscript_folio_383_fragment_recto_and_verso.jpg)

The [Spitzer Manuscript](/wiki/Spitzer_Manuscript "Spitzer Manuscript") is dated to about the 2nd century CE (above: folio 383 fragment). Discovered in the [Kizil Caves](/wiki/Kizil_Caves "Kizil Caves"), near the northern branch of the Central Asian [Silk Route](/wiki/Silk_Route "Silk Route") in northwest China,[[151]](#cite_note-165) it is the oldest Sanskrit philosophical manuscript known so far.[[152]](#cite_note-166)[[153]](#cite_note-167)

The Sanskrit language has been one of the major means for the transmission of knowledge and ideas in Asian history. Indian texts in Sanskrit were already in China by 402 CE, carried by the influential Buddhist pilgrim [Faxian](/wiki/Faxian "Faxian") who translated them into Chinese by 418 CE.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobert_E._Buswell_Jr.Donald_S._Lopez_Jr.2013504-168) [Xuanzang](/wiki/Xuanzang "Xuanzang"), another Chinese Buddhist pilgrim, learnt Sanskrit in India and carried 657 Sanskrit texts to China in the 7th century where he established a major center of learning and language translation under the patronage of Emperor Taizong.[[155]](#cite_note-169)[[156]](#cite_note-170) By the early 1st millennium CE, Sanskrit had spread Buddhist and Hindu ideas to Southeast Asia,[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERamesh_Chandra_Majumdar19741–4-171) parts of the East Asia[[158]](#cite_note-OrzechSørensen2011-172) and the Central Asia.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBanerji1989595–596-173) It was accepted as a language of high culture and the preferred language by some of the local ruling elites in these regions.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMichael_C._Howard201221-174) According to the [Dalai Lama](/wiki/Dalai_Lama "Dalai Lama"), the Sanskrit language is a parent language that is at the foundation of many modern languages of India and the one that promoted Indian thought to other distant countries. In Tibetan Buddhism, states the Dalai Lama, Sanskrit language has been a revered one and called *legjar lhai-ka* or "elegant language of the gods". It has been the means of transmitting the "profound wisdom of Buddhist philosophy" to Tibet.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThe_Fourteenth_Dalai_Lama19793–5-175)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/One_of_earliest_Sanskrit_inscriptions_in_Java_Indonesia.jpg/500px-One_of_earliest_Sanskrit_inscriptions_in_Java_Indonesia.jpg)](/wiki/File:One_of_earliest_Sanskrit_inscriptions_in_Java_Indonesia.jpg)

A 5th-century [Sanskrit inscription](/wiki/Sanskrit_inscriptions_in_the_Malay_world "Sanskrit inscriptions in the Malay world") discovered in [Java](/wiki/Java "Java"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia")—one of the earliest in southeast Asia after the [Mulavarman inscription](/wiki/Y%C5%ABpa#Yūpa_inscription_in_Indonesia "Yūpa") discovered in [Kutai](/wiki/Kutai "Kutai"), eastern [Borneo](/wiki/Borneo "Borneo"). The [Ciaruteun inscription](/wiki/Ciaruteun_inscription "Ciaruteun inscription") combines two writing scripts and compares the king to the Hindu god [Vishnu](/wiki/Vishnu "Vishnu"). It provides a *[terminus ad quem](/wiki/Terminus_ad_quem "Terminus ad quem")* to the presence of Hinduism in the Indonesian islands. The oldest southeast Asian Sanskrit inscription—called the [Vo Canh inscription](/wiki/V%C3%B5_C%E1%BA%A1nh_inscription "Võ Cạnh inscription")—so far discovered is near [Nha Trang](/wiki/Nha_Trang "Nha Trang"), [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"), and it is dated to the late 4th century to early 5th century CE.[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasica199355–56-176)[[163]](#cite_note-177)

The Sanskrit language created a pan-Indo-Aryan accessibility to information and knowledge in the ancient and medieval times, in contrast to the Prakrit languages which were understood just regionally.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeshpande2011222–223-134)[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurrow197360-178) It created a cultural bond across the subcontinent.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurrow197360-178) As local languages and dialects evolved and diversified, Sanskrit served as the common language.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurrow197360-178) It connected scholars from distant parts of South Asia such as Tamil Nadu and Kashmir, states Deshpande, as well as those from different fields of studies, though there must have been differences in its pronunciation given the first language of the respective speakers. The Sanskrit language brought Indo-Aryan speaking people together, particularly its elite scholars.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeshpande2011222–223-134) Some of these scholars of Indian history regionally produced vernacularized Sanskrit to reach wider audiences, as evidenced by texts discovered in Rajasthan, Gujarat, and Maharashtra. Once the audience became familiar with the easier to understand vernacularized version of Sanskrit, those interested could graduate from colloquial Sanskrit to the more advanced Classical Sanskrit. Rituals and the rites-of-passage ceremonies have been and continue to be the other occasions where a wide spectrum of people hear Sanskrit, and occasionally join in to speak some Sanskrit words such as *namah*.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeshpande2011222–223-134)

Classical Sanskrit is the standard [register](/wiki/Register_(sociolinguistics) "Register (sociolinguistics)") as laid out in the grammar of *[Pāṇini](/wiki/P%C4%81%E1%B9%87ini "Pāṇini")*, around the fourth century BCE.[[165]](#cite_note-Houben_1996_p.-179) Its position in the cultures of [Greater India](/wiki/Greater_India "Greater India") is akin to that of [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") and [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") in Europe. Sanskrit has significantly influenced most modern languages of the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"), particularly the languages of the northern, western, central and eastern Indian subcontinent.[[166]](#cite_note-Bright2014p16-180)[[167]](#cite_note-Groff2017-181)[[168]](#cite_note-Pandey2015p86-182)

### Decline

The decline of Sanskrit began in the 13th century.[[125]](#cite_note-pollock1996p197-136)[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHock1983-183) This coincides with the beginning of [Islamic](/wiki/Islam "Islam") invasions of South Asia to create, and thereafter expand the [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") rule in the form of Sultanates, and later the [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire").[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESheldon_Pollock2009167–168-184) Sheldon Pollock characterises the decline of Sanskrit as a long-term "cultural, social, and political change". He dismisses the idea that Sanskrit declined due to "struggle with barbarous invaders", and emphasises factors such as the increasing attractiveness of vernacular language for literary expression.[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPollock2001-185)

With the fall of [Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir "Kashmir") around the 13th century, a premier center of Sanskrit literary creativity, Sanskrit literature there disappeared,[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHanneder2002293–310-186) perhaps in the "fires that periodically engulfed the capital of Kashmir" or the "Mongol invasion of 1320" states Pollock.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPollock2001397–398-187) The dissemination of Sanskrit literature from the northwest regions of the subcontinent stopped after the 12th century.[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPollock2001398-188) As Hindu kingdoms fell in eastern and southern India, such as the great [Vijayanagara Empire](/wiki/Vijayanagara_Empire "Vijayanagara Empire"), so did Sanskrit.[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHanneder2002293–310-186) There were exceptions and short periods of imperial support for Sanskrit, mostly concentrated during the reign of the tolerant Mughal emperor [Akbar](/wiki/Akbar "Akbar").[[175]](#cite_note-Truschke2016p9-189) Muslim rulers patronized the Middle Eastern language and scripts found in Persia and Arabia, and the Indians linguistically adapted to this Persianization to gain employment with the Muslim rulers.[[176]](#cite_note-Deshpande1993p118-190) Hindu rulers such as Shivaji of the [Maratha Empire](/wiki/Maratha_Empire "Maratha Empire"), reversed the process, by re-adopting Sanskrit and re-asserting their socio-linguistic identity.[[176]](#cite_note-Deshpande1993p118-190)[[177]](#cite_note-191)[[178]](#cite_note-192) After Islamic rule disintegrated in South Asia and the colonial rule era began, Sanskrit re-emerged but in the form of a "ghostly existence" in regions such as Bengal. This decline was the result of "political institutions and civic ethos" that did not support the historic Sanskrit literary culture[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHanneder2002293–310-186) and the failure of new Sanskrit literature to assimilate into the changing cultural and political environment.[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPollock2001-185)

[Sheldon Pollock](/wiki/Sheldon_Pollock "Sheldon Pollock") states that in some crucial way, "Sanskrit is [dead](/wiki/Language_death "Language death")".[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPollock2001393-193) After the 12th century, the Sanskrit literary works were reduced to "reinscription and restatements" of ideas already explored, and any creativity was restricted to hymns and verses. This contrasted with the previous 1,500 years when "great experiments in moral and aesthetic imagination" marked the Indian scholarship using Classical Sanskrit, states Pollock.[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPollock2001398-188)

Some scholars maintain that the Sanskrit language did not die, but rather only declined. Jurgen Hanneder disagrees with Pollock, finding his arguments elegant but "often arbitrary". According to Hanneder, a decline or regional absence of creative and innovative literature constitutes a negative evidence to Pollock's hypothesis, but it is not positive evidence. A closer look at Sanskrit in the Indian history after the 12th century suggests that Sanskrit survived despite the odds. According to Hanneder,[[180]](#cite_note-Hatcher_2007_pp._333_–_361-194)

> On a more public level the statement that Sanskrit is a dead language is misleading, for Sanskrit is quite obviously not as dead as other dead languages and the fact that it is spoken, written and read will probably convince most people that it cannot be a dead language in the most common usage of the term. Pollock's notion of the "death of Sanskrit" remains in this unclear realm between academia and public opinion when he says that "most observers would agree that, in some crucial way, Sanskrit is dead."[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHanneder2002293–310-186)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Isha_Upanishad_Verses_1_to_3%2C_Shukla_Yajurveda%2C_Sanskrit%2C_Devanagari.jpg/330px-Isha_Upanishad_Verses_1_to_3%2C_Shukla_Yajurveda%2C_Sanskrit%2C_Devanagari.jpg)](/wiki/File:Isha_Upanishad_Verses_1_to_3,_Shukla_Yajurveda,_Sanskrit,_Devanagari.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/1863_CE_palm_leaf_manuscript%2C_Jaiminiya_Aranyaka_Gana%2C_Samaveda_%28unidentified_layer_of_texts%29%2C_Sanskrit%2C_Southern_Grantha_script%2C_Malayali_scribe_Kecavan%2C_sample_ii.jpg/330px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:1863_CE_palm_leaf_manuscript,_Jaiminiya_Aranyaka_Gana,_Samaveda_(unidentified_layer_of_texts),_Sanskrit,_Southern_Grantha_script,_Malayali_scribe_Kecavan,_sample_ii.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/3rd_Chapter_Verses_1-2%2C_Bhagavad_Gita%2C_Gurmukhi_script%2C_Punjab.jpg/330px-3rd_Chapter_Verses_1-2%2C_Bhagavad_Gita%2C_Gurmukhi_script%2C_Punjab.jpg)](/wiki/File:3rd_Chapter_Verses_1-2,_Bhagavad_Gita,_Gurmukhi_script,_Punjab.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Vedanta_Sara_manuscript%2C_page_4_recto%2C_Sanskrit%2C_Telugu_script.jpg/330px-Vedanta_Sara_manuscript%2C_page_4_recto%2C_Sanskrit%2C_Telugu_script.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vedanta_Sara_manuscript,_page_4_recto,_Sanskrit,_Telugu_script.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Manuscript_fragment_of_the_Buddhist_Jatakamala%2C_Sanskrit_language_in_the_Gilgit-Bamiyan-Typ_II_Protosarada_script%2C_Toyuk%2C_probably_8th-9th_century_-_Ethnological_Museum%2C_Berlin_-_DSC01754.JPG/330px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Manuscript_fragment_of_the_Buddhist_Jatakamala,_Sanskrit_language_in_the_Gilgit-Bamiyan-Typ_II_Protosarada_script,_Toyuk,_probably_8th-9th_century_-_Ethnological_Museum,_Berlin_-_DSC01754.JPG)

Sanskrit language manuscripts exist in many scripts. Above from top: *Isha Upanishad* (Devanagari), *Samaveda* (Tamil Grantha), *Bhagavad Gita* (Gurmukhi), *Vedanta Sara* (Telugu), *Jatakamala* (early Sharada). All are Hindu texts except the last Buddhist text.

The Sanskrit language scholar [Moriz Winternitz](/wiki/Moriz_Winternitz "Moriz Winternitz") states that Sanskrit was never a dead language and it is still alive though its prevalence is lesser than ancient and medieval times. Sanskrit remains an integral part of Hindu journals, festivals, Ramlila plays, drama, rituals and the rites-of-passage.[[181]](#cite_note-195) Similarly, Brian Hatcher states that the "metaphors of historical rupture" by Pollock are not valid, that there is ample proof that Sanskrit was very much alive in the narrow confines of surviving Hindu kingdoms between the 13th and 18th centuries, and its reverence and tradition continues.[[180]](#cite_note-Hatcher_2007_pp._333_–_361-194)

Hanneder states that modern works in Sanskrit are either ignored or their "modernity" contested.[[182]](#cite_note-196)

According to [Robert P. Goldman](/wiki/Robert_P._Goldman "Robert P. Goldman") and Sally Sutherland, Sanskrit is neither "dead" nor "living" in the conventional sense. It is a special, timeless language that lives in the numerous manuscripts, daily chants, and ceremonial recitations, a *heritage language* that Indians contextually prize, and which some practice.[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobert_P._GoldmanSally_J_Sutherland_Goldman2002xi–xii-197)

When the British introduced English to India in the 19th century, knowledge of Sanskrit and ancient literature continued to flourish as the study of Sanskrit changed from a more traditional style into a form of analytical and comparative scholarship mirroring that of Europe.[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESeth2007172–176-198)

### Modern Indo-Aryan languages

The relationship of Sanskrit to the Prakrit languages, particularly the modern form of Indian languages, is complex and spans about 3,500 years, states [Colin Masica](/wiki/Colin_Masica "Colin Masica")—a linguist specializing in South Asian languages. A part of the difficulty is the lack of sufficient textual, archaeological and epigraphical evidence for the ancient Prakrit languages with rare exceptions such as Pali, leading to a tendency toward [anachronistic](/wiki/Anachronism "Anachronism") errors.[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasica199350–57-199) Sanskrit and Prakrit languages may be divided into Old Indo-Aryan (1500 BCE – 600 BCE), Middle Indo-Aryan (600 BCE – 1000 CE) and New Indo-Aryan (1000 CE – present), each can further be subdivided into early, middle or second, and late evolutionary substages.[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasica199350–57-199)

Vedic Sanskrit belongs to the early Old Indo-Aryan stage, while Classical Sanskrit to the later Old Indo-Aryan stage. The evidence for Prakrits such as Pali (Theravada Buddhism) and Ardhamagadhi (Jainism), along with Magadhi, Maharashtri, Sinhala, Sauraseni and Niya (Gandhari), emerge in the Middle Indo-Aryan stage in two versions—archaic and more formalized—that may be placed in early and middle substages of the 600 BCE – 1000 CE period.[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasica199350–57-199) Two literary Indo-Aryan languages can be traced to the late Middle Indo-Aryan stage and these are *Apabhramsa* and *Elu* (a literary form of [Sinhalese](/wiki/Sinhala_language "Sinhala language")). Numerous North, Central, Eastern and Western Indian languages, such as Hindi, Gujarati, Sindhi, Punjabi, Kashmiri, Nepali, Braj, Awadhi, Bengali, Assamese, Oriya, Marathi, and others belong to the New Indo-Aryan stage.[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasica199350–57-199)

There is an extensive overlap in the vocabulary, phonetics and other aspects of these New Indo-Aryan languages with Sanskrit, but it is neither universal nor identical across the languages. They likely emerged from a synthesis of the ancient Sanskrit language traditions and an admixture of various regional dialects. Each language has some unique and regionally creative aspects, with unclear origins. Prakrit languages do have a grammatical structure, but like Vedic Sanskrit, it is far less rigorous than Classical Sanskrit. While the roots of all Prakrit languages may be in Vedic Sanskrit and ultimately the Proto-Indo-Aryan language, their structural details vary from Classical Sanskrit.[[30]](#cite_note-Woolner1986p3-34)[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasica199350–57-199) It is generally accepted by scholars and widely believed in India that the modern [Indo-Aryan languages](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages") – such as Bengali, Gujarati, Hindi, and Punjabi – are descendants of the Sanskrit language.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPhilipp_Strazny2013499–500-200)[[187]](#cite_note-201)[[188]](#cite_note-202) Sanskrit, states Burjor Avari, can be described as "the mother language of almost all the languages of north India".[[189]](#cite_note-203)

## Geographic distribution

See also: [Sanskrit inscriptions in the Malay world](/wiki/Sanskrit_inscriptions_in_the_Malay_world "Sanskrit inscriptions in the Malay world")

Further information: [Sanskritisation](/wiki/Sanskritisation "Sanskritisation") and [Indosphere](/wiki/Indosphere "Indosphere")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Global_distribution_of_Sanskrit_language_presence%2C_texts_and_inscriptions_dated_between_300_and_1800_CE.svg/500px-Global_distribution_of_Sanskrit_language_presence%2C_texts_and_inscriptions_dated_between_300_and_1800_CE.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Global_distribution_of_Sanskrit_language_presence,_texts_and_inscriptions_dated_between_300_and_1800_CE.svg)

Sanskrit language's historical presence has been attested in many countries. The evidence includes manuscript pages and inscriptions discovered in South Asia, Southeast Asia and Central Asia. These have been dated between 300 and 1800 CE.

The Sanskrit language's historic presence is attested across a wide geography beyond South Asia. Inscriptions and literary evidence suggests that Sanskrit language was already being adopted in Southeast Asia and Central Asia in the 1st millennium CE, through monks, religious pilgrims and merchants.[[190]](#cite_note-204)[[191]](#cite_note-205)[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurrow197363–66-206)

South Asia has been the geographic range of the largest collection of the ancient and pre-18th-century Sanskrit manuscripts and inscriptions.[[137]](#cite_note-scharf233-148) Beyond ancient India, significant collections of Sanskrit manuscripts and inscriptions have been found in China (particularly the Tibetan monasteries),[[193]](#cite_note-207)[[194]](#cite_note-vergahen159-208) [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"),[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon1998154–155-209) [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"),[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon1998158–159-210) [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia"),[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon1998155–157-211) [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos"),[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon1998158-212) [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"),[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon1998157-213) [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"),[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon1998155-214) and [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia").[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon1998158-212) Sanskrit inscriptions, manuscripts or its remnants, including some of the oldest known Sanskrit written texts, have been discovered in dry high deserts and mountainous terrains such as in Nepal,[[201]](#cite_note-215)[[202]](#cite_note-Lewis343-216)[[o]](#cite_note-217) Tibet,[[194]](#cite_note-vergahen159-208)[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon1998159–160-218) Afghanistan,[[204]](#cite_note-219)[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon1998152–153-220) Mongolia,[[206]](#cite_note-221) Uzbekistan,[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon1998153–154-222) Turkmenistan, Tajikistan,[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon1998153–154-222) and Kazakhstan.[[208]](#cite_note-223) Some Sanskrit texts and inscriptions have also been discovered in Korea and Japan.[[209]](#cite_note-224)[[210]](#cite_note-225)[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon1998160_with_footnote_134-226)

### Official status

See also: [Sanskrit revival](/wiki/Sanskrit_revival "Sanskrit revival") and [Sanskrit universities in India](/wiki/List_of_Sanskrit_universities_in_India "List of Sanskrit universities in India")

In India, Sanskrit is among the 22 official languages of India in [the Eighth Schedule to the Constitution](/wiki/Languages_with_official_status_in_India#Schedule "Languages with official status in India").[[212]](#cite_note-groff2013-227) In 2010, [Uttarakhand](/wiki/Uttarakhand "Uttarakhand") became the first state in India to make Sanskrit its second official language.[[213]](#cite_note-228) In 2019, [Himachal Pradesh](/wiki/Himachal_Pradesh "Himachal Pradesh") made Sanskrit its second official language, becoming the second state in India to do so.[[214]](#cite_note-229)

## Phonology

Sanskrit shares many Proto-Indo-European phonological features, although it features a larger inventory of distinct phonemes. The consonantal system is the same, though it systematically enlarged the inventory of distinct sounds. For example, Sanskrit added a voiceless aspirated "tʰ", to the voiceless "t", voiced "d" and voiced aspirated "dʰ" found in PIE languages.[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison20088–9-230)

The most significant and distinctive phonological development in Sanskrit is vowel merger.[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison20088–9-230) The short *\*e*, *\*o* and *\*a*, all merge as *a* (अ) in Sanskrit, while long *\*ē*, *\*ō* and *\*ā*, all merge as long *ā* (आ). Compare Sanskrit *nāman* to Latin *nōmen*. These mergers occurred very early and significantly affected Sanskrit's morphological system.[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison20088–9-230) Some phonological developments in it mirror those in other PIE languages. For example, the labiovelars merged with the plain velars as in other satem languages. The secondary palatalization of the resulting segments is more thorough and systematic within Sanskrit.[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison20088–9-230) For example, unlike the loss of the morphological clarity from vowel contraction that is found in early Greek and related southeast European languages, Sanskrit deployed *\*y*, *\*w*, and *\*s* intervocalically to provide morphological clarity.[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison20088–9-230)

### Vowels

[![A palm leaf manuscript published in 828 CE with the Sanskrit alphabet](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/828_CE_Paramesvaratantra_Sanskrit_palm_leaf_manuscipt%2C_Late_Gupta_script%2C_Nepal.jpg/500px-828_CE_Paramesvaratantra_Sanskrit_palm_leaf_manuscipt%2C_Late_Gupta_script%2C_Nepal.jpg)](/wiki/File:828_CE_Paramesvaratantra_Sanskrit_palm_leaf_manuscipt,_Late_Gupta_script,_Nepal.jpg "This is one of the oldest surviving and dated palm-leaf manuscripts in Sanskrit (828 CE). Discovered in Nepal, the bottom leaf shows all the vowels and consonants of Sanskrit (the first five consonants are highlighted in blue and yellow).")

This is one of the oldest surviving and dated palm-leaf manuscripts in Sanskrit (828 CE). Discovered in Nepal, the bottom leaf shows all the vowels and consonants of Sanskrit (the first five consonants are highlighted in blue and yellow).

The cardinal vowels (*svaras*) *a* (अ), *i* (इ), and *u* (उ) distinguish length in Sanskrit.[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison20089-231)[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobert_P._GoldmanSally_J_Sutherland_Goldman20021–9-232) The short *a* (अ) in Sanskrit is a closer vowel than ā, equivalent to schwa. The mid vowels ē (ए) and ō (ओ) in Sanskrit are monophthongizations of the Indo-Iranian diphthongs *\*ai* and *\*au*. They are inherently long, though often transcribed *e* and *o* without the diacritic. The vocalic liquid *r̥* in Sanskrit is a merger of PIE *\*r̥* and *\*l̥*. The long *r̥* is an innovation and it is used in a few analogically generated morphological categories.[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison20089-231)[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMichael_CoulsonRichard_GombrichJames_Benson201121–36-233)[[219]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasica1993163–165-234)

According to Masica, Sanskrit has four traditional semivowels, with which were classed, "for morphophonemic reasons, the liquids: y, r, l, and v; that is, as y and v were the non-syllabics corresponding to i, u, so were r, l in relation to r̥ and l̥".[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasica1993160–161-241) The northwestern, the central and the eastern Sanskrit dialects have had a historic confusion between "r" and "l". The Paninian system that followed the central dialect preserved the distinction, likely out of reverence for the Vedic Sanskrit that distinguished the "r" and "l". However, the northwestern dialect only had "r", while the eastern dialect probably only had "l", states Masica. Thus literary works from different parts of ancient India appear inconsistent in their use of "r" and "l", resulting in doublets that are occasionally semantically differentiated.[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasica1993160–161-241)

The nasal 'ṃ' is optionally the corresponding nasal consonant before plosives (aṃ + k = aṅk or am k) and an 'm'-sound before r, s, ś, ṣ, and h. Before y, l, v, it can cause nasalaization and gemination (am + y = aỹy or am y).[[225]](#cite_note-242)

### Consonants

Sanskrit consonants are conventionally arranged into a symmetrical table that is based on how the sound is articulated, though this arrangement is somewhat forced and conceals a certain lack of parallelism among the actual sounds.[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison20089–10-243)
