> **The system of Sanskrit Sounds**  
> [The alphabetic] order of Sanskrit sounds works along three principles: it goes from simple to complex; it goes from the back to the front of the mouth; and it groups similar sounds together. [...] Among themselves, both the vowels and consonants are ordered according to where in the mouth they are pronounced, going from back to front.

— A. M. Ruppel, *The Cambridge Introduction to Sanskrit*[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEA._M._Ruppel201718–19-244)

* Sanskrit has a series of retroflex stops originating as conditioned alternants of dentals.[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison20089–10-243)
* *jh* is a marginal phoneme in Sanskrit occurring mostly from loanwords, hence its phonology is more difficult to reconstruct; it was more commonly employed in the Middle Indo-Aryan languages as a result of phonological processes resulting in the phoneme, e.g. [jajjhatī](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E0%A4%9C%E0%A4%9C%E0%A5%8D%E0%A4%9D%E0%A4%A4%E0%A5%80 "wikt:जज्झती") [jhaṣa](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E0%A4%9D%E0%A4%B7 "wikt:झष").
* The palatal and velar nasals are conditioned variant of n occurring next to palatal and velar obstruents mostly,[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison20089–10-243) but there are some onomatopoeic words with them like *ña, ṅa, ṅu* and inflections like *[ñuṇūṣate](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E0%A4%9E%E0%A5%81%E0%A4%A3%E0%A5%82%E0%A4%B7%E0%A4%A4%E0%A5%87 "wikt:ञुणूषते")*, *[prāṅ](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E0%A4%AA%E0%A5%8D%E0%A4%B0%E0%A4%BE%E0%A4%99%E0%A5%8D "wikt:प्राङ्")*.
* The *anusvara* that Sanskrit deploys is a conditioned alternant of post-vocalic nasals, under certain sandhi conditions.[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200810-245)
* The *visarga* is a word-final or morpheme-final conditioned alternant of s and r under certain sandhi conditions.[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200810-245)
* The voiceless aspirated series is also an innovation in Sanskrit but is rarer than the other three series.[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison20089–10-243)
* While the Sanskrit language organizes sounds for expression beyond those found in the PIE language, it retained many features found in the Iranian and Balto-Slavic languages. An example of a similar process in all three is the retroflex sibilant ʂ being the automatic product of dental s [following i, u, r, and k](/wiki/Ruki_sound_law "Ruki sound law").[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200810-245)

### Phonological alternations, sandhi rules

See also: [Sandhi](/wiki/Sandhi "Sandhi") and [Sanskrit grammar](/wiki/Sanskrit_grammar "Sanskrit grammar")

Sanskrit deploys extensive phonological alternations on different linguistic levels through *sandhi* rules (literally, the rules of "putting together, union, connection, alliance"), similar to the English alteration of "going to" as *gonna*.[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200810–11-246) The Sanskrit language accepts such alterations within it, but offers formal rules for the *sandhi* of any two words next to each other in the same sentence or linking two sentences. The external *sandhi* rules state that similar short vowels coalesce into a single long vowel, while dissimilar vowels form glides or undergo diphthongization.[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200810–11-246) Among the consonants, most external *sandhi* rules recommend regressive assimilation for clarity when they are voiced. These rules ordinarily apply at compound seams and morpheme boundaries.[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200810–11-246) In Vedic Sanskrit, the external *sandhi* rules are more variable than in Classical Sanskrit.[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200811-247)

### Vedic Pitch Accent

See also: [Vedic accent](/wiki/Vedic_accent "Vedic accent") and [Vedic chant](/wiki/Vedic_chant "Vedic chant")

Vedic Sanskrit included a three-way pitch accent system of *udātta* (raised), *anudātta* (not-raised or grave), and *svarita* (sounded) which were high, low and falling pitches respectively. Each word generally had one *udātta* accent except for certain compound words. The classical language ultimately lost this system, but it was preserved in Vedic texts and phonological treatises. Traditional chanting renders the *udātta* as an unaccented mid-tone and the first half of the *svarita* as higher than an *udātta*; the other half is rendered the same level of that of an *udātta.*[[231]](#cite_note-248)

## Morphology

Main article: [Sanskrit grammar](/wiki/Sanskrit_grammar "Sanskrit grammar")

See also: [Vedic Sanskrit grammar](/wiki/Vedic_Sanskrit_grammar "Vedic Sanskrit grammar") and [Sanskrit verbs](/wiki/Sanskrit_verbs "Sanskrit verbs")

The basis of Sanskrit morphology is the root, states Jamison, "a morpheme bearing lexical meaning".[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200815-249) The verbal and nominal stems of Sanskrit words are derived from this root through the phonological vowel-gradation processes, the addition of affixes, verbal and nominal stems. It then adds an ending to establish the grammatical and syntactic identity of the stem. According to Jamison, the "three major formal elements of the morphology are (i) root, (ii) affix, and (iii) ending; and they are roughly responsible for (i) lexical meaning, (ii) derivation, and (iii) inflection respectively".[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200815–16-250)

A Sanskrit word has the following canonical structure:[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200815-249)

*Root* + *Affix*  
0-n + *Ending*  
0–1

The root structure has certain phonological constraints. Two of the most important constraints of a "root" is that it does not end in a short "a" (अ) and that it is monosyllabic.[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200815-249) In contrast, the affixes and endings commonly do. The affixes in Sanskrit are almost always suffixes, with exceptions such as the augment "a-" added as prefix to past tense verb forms and the "-na/n-" infix in single verbal present class, states Jamison.[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200815-249)

[Sanskrit verbs](/wiki/Sanskrit_verbs "Sanskrit verbs") have the following canonical structure:[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200820-251)

*Root* + *Suffix*  
Tense-Aspect + *Suffix*  
Mood + *Ending*  
Personal-Number-Voice

According to Ruppel, verbs in Sanskrit express the same information as other Indo-European languages such as English.[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEA._M._Ruppel201731–33-252) Sanskrit verbs describe an action or occurrence or state, its embedded morphology informs as to "who is doing it" (person or persons), "when it is done" (tense) and "how it is done" (mood, voice). The Indo-European languages differ in the detail. For example, the Sanskrit language attaches the affixes and ending to the verb root, while the English language adds small independent words before the verb. In Sanskrit, these elements co-exist within the word.[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEA._M._Ruppel201731–33-252)[[r]](#cite_note-254)

Word morphology in Sanskrit, A. M. Ruppel[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEA._M._Ruppel201731–33-252)[[s]](#cite_note-255)

|  | Sanskrit word equivalent | |
| --- | --- | --- |
| English expression | [IAST](/wiki/IAST "IAST")/ISO | [Devanagari](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari") |
| you carry | *bharasi* | भरसि |
| they carry | *bharanti* | भरन्ति |
| you will carry | *bhariṣyasi* | भरिष्यसि |

Both verbs and nouns in Sanskrit are either thematic or athematic, states Jamison.[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200819–20-256) *Guna* (strengthened) forms in the active singular regularly alternate in athematic verbs. The finite verbs of Classical Sanskrit have the following grammatical categories: person, number, voice, tense-aspect, and mood. According to Jamison, a portmanteau morpheme generally expresses the person-number-voice in Sanskrit, and sometimes also the ending or only the ending. The mood of the word is embedded in the affix.[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200819–20-256)

These elements of word architecture are the typical building blocks in Classical Sanskrit, but in Vedic Sanskrit these elements fluctuate and are unclear. For example, in the *Rigveda* [preverbs](/wiki/Preverb "Preverb") regularly occur in [tmesis](/wiki/Tmesis "Tmesis"), states Jamison, which means they are "separated from the finite verb".[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200815-249) This indecisiveness is likely linked to Vedic Sanskrit's attempt to incorporate accent. With nonfinite forms of the verb and with nominal derivatives thereof, states Jamison, "preverbs show much clearer univerbation in Vedic, both by position and by accent, and by Classical Sanskrit, tmesis is no longer possible even with finite forms".[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200815-249)

While roots are typical in Sanskrit, some words do not follow the canonical structure.[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200815–16-250) A few forms lack both inflection and root. Many words are inflected (and can enter into derivation) but lack a recognizable root. Examples from the basic vocabulary include kinship terms such as *mātar-* (mother), *nas-* (nose), *śvan-* (dog). According to Jamison, pronouns and some words outside the semantic categories also lack roots, as do the numerals. Similarly, the Sanskrit language is flexible enough to not mandate inflection.[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200815–16-250)

The Sanskrit words can contain more than one affix that interact with each other. Affixes in Sanskrit can be athematic as well as thematic, according to Jamison.[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200816–17-257) Athematic affixes can be alternating. Sanskrit deploys eight cases, namely nominative, accusative, instrumental, dative, ablative, genitive, locative, vocative.[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200816–17-257)

Stems, that is "root + affix", appear in two categories in Sanskrit: vowel stems and consonant stems. Unlike some Indo-European languages such as Latin or Greek, according to Jamison, "Sanskrit has no closed set of conventionally denoted noun declensions". Sanskrit includes a fairly large set of stem-types.[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200817–18-258) The linguistic interaction of the roots, the phonological segments, lexical items and the grammar for the Classical Sanskrit consist of four *Paninian* components. These, states Paul Kiparsky, are the *Astadhyaayi*, a comprehensive system of 4,000 grammatical rules, of which a small set are frequently used; *Sivasutras*, an inventory of *anubandhas* (markers) that partition phonological segments for efficient abbreviations through the *pratyharas* technique; *Dhatupatha*, a list of 2,000 verbal roots classified by their morphology and syntactic properties using diacritic markers, a structure that guides its writing systems; and, the *Ganapatha*, an inventory of word groups, classes of lexical systems.[[240]](#cite_note-Kiparsky59-259) There are peripheral adjuncts to these four, such as the *Unadisutras*, which focus on irregularly formed derivatives from the roots.[[240]](#cite_note-Kiparsky59-259)

Sanskrit morphology is generally studied in two broad fundamental categories: the nominal forms and the verbal forms. These differ in the types of endings and what these endings mark in the grammatical context.[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200815–16-250) Pronouns and nouns share the same grammatical categories, though they may differ in inflection. Verb-based adjectives and participles are not formally distinct from nouns. Adverbs are typically frozen case forms of adjectives, states Jamison, and "nonfinite verbal forms such as infinitives and gerunds also clearly show frozen nominal case endings".[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200815–16-250)

### Verbal forms

The Sanskrit language includes five tenses: present, future, past imperfect, past [aorist](/wiki/Aorist "Aorist") and past perfect.[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEA._M._Ruppel201733–34-253) It outlines three types of voices: active, passive and the middle.[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEA._M._Ruppel201733–34-253) The middle is also referred to as the mediopassive, or more formally in Sanskrit as *parasmaipada* (word for another) and *atmanepada* (word for oneself).[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200820-251)

Voice in Sanskrit, Stephanie Jamison[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200820-251)[[t]](#cite_note-260)

|  | Active | | | Middle (Mediopassive) | | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Singular | Dual | Plural | Singular | Dual | Plural |
| 1st person | *-mi* | *-vaḥ* | *-maḥ* | *-e* | *-vahe* | *-mahe* |
| 2nd person | *-si* | *-thaḥ* | *-tha* | *-se* | *-āthe* | *-dhve* |
| 3rd person | *-ti* | *-taḥ* | *-anti* | *-te* | *-āte* | *-ante* |

The paradigm for the tense-aspect system in Sanskrit is the three-way contrast between the "present", the "aorist" and the "perfect" architecture.[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200821-261) Vedic Sanskrit is more elaborate and had several additional tenses. For example, the *Rigveda* includes perfect and a marginal pluperfect. Classical Sanskrit simplifies the "present" system down to two tenses, the perfect and the imperfect, while the "aorist" stems retain the aorist tense and the "perfect" stems retain the perfect and marginal pluperfect.[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200821-261) The classical version of the language has elaborate rules for both voice and the tense-aspect system to emphasize clarity, and this is more elaborate than in other Indo-European languages. The evolution of these systems can be seen from the earliest layers of the Vedic literature to the late Vedic literature.[[242]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200820–21-262)

The three verbal moods in Sanskrit are indicative, potential (optative), and imperative.[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEA._M._Ruppel201733–34-253)

### Nominal forms

Sanskrit recognizes three numbers—singular, dual, and plural.[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200816–17-257) The dual is a fully functioning category, used beyond naturally paired objects such as hands or eyes, extending to any collection of two. The elliptical dual is notable in the Vedic Sanskrit, according to Jamison, where a noun in the dual signals a paired opposition.[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200816–17-257) Illustrations include *dyāvā* (literally, "the two heavens" for heaven-and-earth), *mātarā* (literally, "the two mothers" for mother-and-father).[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200816–17-257) A verb may be singular, dual or plural, while the person recognized in the language are forms of "I", "you", "he/she/it", "we" and "they".[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEA._M._Ruppel201733–34-253)

There are three persons in Sanskrit: first, second and third.[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200820-251) Sanskrit uses the 3×3 grid formed by the three numbers and the three persons parameters as the paradigm and the basic building block of its verbal system.[[242]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200820–21-262)

The Sanskrit language incorporates three genders: feminine, masculine and neuter.[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200816–17-257) All nouns have inherent gender. With some exceptions, personal pronouns have no gender. Exceptions include demonstrative and anaphoric pronouns.[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200816–17-257) Derivation of a word is used to express the feminine. Two most common derivations come from feminine-forming suffixes, the *-ā-* (आ, Rādhā) and *-ī-* (ई, Rukminī). The masculine and neuter are much simpler, and the difference between them is primarily inflectional.[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200816–17-257)[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERobert_P._GoldmanSally_J_Sutherland_Goldman200259,_79,_91,_113-263) Similar affixes for the feminine are found in many Indo-European languages, states Burrow, suggesting links of the Sanskrit to its PIE heritage.[[244]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurrow1973191–194-264)

Pronouns in Sanskrit include the personal pronouns of the first and second persons, unmarked for gender, and a larger number of gender-distinguishing pronouns and adjectives.[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200819–20-256) Examples of the former include *ahám* (first singular), *vayám* (first plural) and *yūyám* (second plural). The latter can be demonstrative, deictic or anaphoric.[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200819–20-256) Both the Vedic and Classical Sanskrit share the *sá/tám* pronominal stem, and this is the closest element to a third person pronoun and an article in the Sanskrit language, states Jamison.[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamison200819–20-256)

### Prosody, metre

Main articles: [Sanskrit prosody](/wiki/Sanskrit_prosody "Sanskrit prosody") and [Vedic metre](/wiki/Vedic_metre "Vedic metre")

The Sanskrit language formally incorporates [poetic metres](/wiki/Metre_(poetry) "Metre (poetry)").[[245]](#cite_note-jameslochtefeldsca140-265) By the late Vedic era, this developed into a field of study; it was central to the composition of the Hindu literature, including the later Vedic texts. This study of Sanskrit prosody is called *[chandas](/wiki/Sanskrit_prosody "Sanskrit prosody")*, and is considered one of the six [Vedangas](/wiki/Vedanga "Vedanga"), or limbs of Vedic studies.[[245]](#cite_note-jameslochtefeldsca140-265)[[246]](#cite_note-266)

> There is no word without metre,  
> nor is there any metre without words.

— *Natya Shastra*[[247]](#cite_note-267)

Sanskrit metres include those based on a fixed number of syllables per verse, and those based on fixed number of [morae](/wiki/Morae "Morae") per verse.[[248]](#cite_note-Allan2013p228-268) The Vedic Sanskrit employs fifteen metres, of which seven are common, and the most frequent are three (8-, 11- and 12-syllable lines).[[249]](#cite_note-PremingerWarnke2015p394-269) The Classical Sanskrit deploys both linear and non-linear metres, many of which are based on syllables and others based on diligently crafted verses based on repeating numbers of morae (matra per foot).[[249]](#cite_note-PremingerWarnke2015p394-269)

## Writing system

Further information: [Brahmi script](/wiki/Brahmi_script "Brahmi script") and [Devanagari](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/828_CE_Sanskrit_manuscript_page%2C_Gupta_script%2C_Nepal%2C_P%C4%81rame%C5%9Bvaratantra_%28MS_Add.1049.1%29.jpg/250px-828_CE_Sanskrit_manuscript_page%2C_Gupta_script%2C_Nepal%2C_P%C4%81rame%C5%9Bvaratantra_%28MS_Add.1049.1%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:828_CE_Sanskrit_manuscript_page,_Gupta_script,_Nepal,_P%C4%81rame%C5%9Bvaratantra_(MS_Add.1049.1).jpg)

One of the oldest surviving Sanskrit manuscript pages in Gupta script (c. 828 CE), discovered in [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal")

The early history of writing Sanskrit and other languages in ancient India is a problematic topic despite a century of scholarship, states [Richard Salomon](/wiki/Richard_G._Salomon_(professor_of_Asian_studies) "Richard G. Salomon (professor of Asian studies)") – an epigraphist and Indologist specializing in Sanskrit and Pali literature.[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon199810-270) The earliest possible script from South Asia is from the [Indus Valley civilization](/wiki/Indus_Valley_civilization "Indus Valley civilization") (3rd/2nd millennium BCE), but this script – if it is a script – remains undeciphered. If any scripts existed in the Vedic period, they have not survived. Scholars generally accept that Sanskrit was spoken in an oral society, and that an [oral tradition](/wiki/Oral_tradition "Oral tradition") preserved the extensive Vedic and Classical Sanskrit literature.[[251]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon19987–10,_86-271) Other scholars such as Jack Goody argue that the Vedic Sanskrit texts are not the product of an oral society, basing this view by comparing inconsistencies in the transmitted versions of literature from various oral societies such as the Greek (Greco-Sanskrit), Serbian, and other cultures. This minority of scholars argue that the Vedic literature is too consistent and vast to have been composed and transmitted orally across generations, without having been written down.[[252]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoody1987110–121-272)[[253]](#cite_note-lopez1995p21-273)[[254]](#cite_note-njallen-274)

*[Lipi](/wiki/Lipi_(script) "Lipi (script)")* is the term in Sanskrit which means "writing, letters, alphabet". It contextually refers to scripts, the art or any manner of writing or drawing.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon199811-109) The term, in the sense of a writing system, appears in some of the earliest Buddhist, Hindu, and Jaina texts. [Pāṇini](/wiki/P%C4%81%E1%B9%87ini "Pāṇini")'s *Astadhyayi*, composed sometime around the 5th or 4th century BCE, for example, mentions *lipi* in the context of a writing script and education system in his times, but he does not name the script.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon199811-109)[[100]](#cite_note-Juhyung_Rhi_2009_5,_1–13-110)[[255]](#cite_note-275) Several early Buddhist and Jaina texts, such as the *[Lalitavistara Sūtra](/wiki/Lalitavistara_S%C5%ABtra "Lalitavistara Sūtra")* and *Pannavana Sutta* include lists of numerous writing scripts in ancient India.[[u]](#cite_note-277) The Buddhist texts list the sixty four *lipi* that the Buddha knew as a child, with the Brahmi script topping the list. "The historical value of this list is however limited by several factors", states Salomon. The list may be a later interpolation.[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon19988–9_with_footnotes-278)[[v]](#cite_note-280) The [Jain](/wiki/Jainism "Jainism") canonical texts such as the *Pannavana Sutta* – probably older than the Buddhist texts – list eighteen writing systems, with the Brahmi topping the list and Kharotthi (Kharoshthi) listed as fourth. The Jaina text elsewhere states that the "Brahmi is written in 18 different forms", but the details are lacking.[[259]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon1998-281) However, the reliability of these lists has been questioned and the empirical evidence of writing systems in the form of Sanskrit or Prakrit inscriptions dated prior to the 3rd century BCE has not been found. If the ancient surfaces for writing Sanskrit were palm leaves, tree bark and cloth – the same as those in later times – these have not survived.[[260]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon19988–14-282)[[w]](#cite_note-284) According to Salomon, many find it difficult to explain the "evidently high level of political organization and cultural complexity" of ancient India without a writing system for Sanskrit and other languages.[[260]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon19988–14-282)[[x]](#cite_note-286)

The oldest datable writing systems for Sanskrit are the [Brāhmī script](/wiki/Brahmi_script "Brahmi script"), the related [Kharoṣṭhī script](/wiki/Kharosthi "Kharosthi") and the Brahmi derivatives.[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaniels1996373–374,_376–378-287)[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon199814–16-288) The Kharosthi was used in the northwestern part of South Asia and it became extinct, while the Brahmi was used all over the subcontinent along with regional scripts such as Old Tamil.[[265]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaniels1996373–375-289) Of these, the earliest records in the Sanskrit language are in Brahmi, a script that later evolved into numerous related Indic scripts for Sanskrit, along with Southeast Asian scripts (Burmese, Thai, Lao, Khmer, others) and many extinct Central Asian scripts such as those discovered along with the [Kharosthi](/wiki/Kharosthi "Kharosthi") in the Tarim Basin of western China and in [Uzbekistan](/wiki/Uzbekistan "Uzbekistan").[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaniels1996373–376-290) The most extensive inscriptions that have survived into the modern era are the rock edicts and pillar inscriptions of the 3rd century BCE Mauryan emperor [Ashoka](/wiki/Ashoka "Ashoka"), but these are not in Sanskrit.[[267]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaniels1996373–374-291)[[y]](#cite_note-292)

### Scripts

Over the centuries, and across countries, a number of scripts have been used to write Sanskrit.

#### Brahmi script

Main article: [Brahmi script](/wiki/Brahmi_script "Brahmi script")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Hathibada_Brahmi_Inscription_at_Nagari%2C_Hinduism_Sanskrit_India.jpg/330px-Hathibada_Brahmi_Inscription_at_Nagari%2C_Hinduism_Sanskrit_India.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hathibada_Brahmi_Inscription_at_Nagari,_Hinduism_Sanskrit_India.jpg)

One of the oldest Hindu Sanskrit[[z]](#cite_note-293) inscriptions, the broken pieces of this early-1st-century BCE [Hathibada Brahmi Inscription](/wiki/Hathibada_Ghosundi_Inscriptions "Hathibada Ghosundi Inscriptions") were discovered in Rajasthan. It is a dedication to deities [Vāsudeva](/wiki/V%C4%81sudeva "Vāsudeva")-[Samkarshana](/wiki/Samkarshana "Samkarshana") ([Krishna](/wiki/Krishna "Krishna")-[Balarama](/wiki/Balarama "Balarama")) and mentions a stone temple.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon199886–87-152)[[268]](#cite_note-294)

The Brahmi script for writing Sanskrit is a "modified consonant-syllabic" script. The graphic syllable is its basic unit, and this consists of a consonant with or without diacritic modifications.[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon199814–16-288) Since the vowel is an integral part of the consonants, and given the efficiently compacted, fused consonant cluster morphology for Sanskrit words and grammar, the Brahmi and its derivative writing systems deploy ligatures, diacritics and relative positioning of the vowel to inform the reader how the vowel is related to the consonant and how it is expected to be pronounced for clarity.[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon199814–16-288)[[269]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaniels1996376–380-295)[[aa]](#cite_note-296) This feature of Brahmi and its modern Indic script derivatives makes it difficult to classify it under the main script types used for the writing systems for most of the world's languages, namely logographic, syllabic and alphabetic.[[264]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon199814–16-288)

Main articles: [Devanagari](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari"), [Nandinagari](/wiki/Nandinagari "Nandinagari"), and [Nāgarī script](/wiki/N%C4%81gar%C4%AB_script "Nāgarī script")

Many modern era manuscripts are written and available in the Nagari script, whose form is attestable to the 1st millennium CE.[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDhanesh_JainGeorge_Cardona200768–70_in_Chapter_3_by_Salomon-297) The Nagari script is the ancestor of [Devanagari](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari") (north India), [Nandinagari](/wiki/Nandinagari "Nandinagari") (south India) and other variants. The Nāgarī script was in regular use by 7th century CE, and had fully evolved into Devanagari and Nandinagari[[271]](#cite_note-298) scripts by about the end of the first millennium of the common era.[[272]](#cite_note-kathleen-299)[[273]](#cite_note-300) The Devanagari script, states Banerji, became more popular for Sanskrit in India since about the 18th century.[[274]](#cite_note-301) However, Sanskrit does have special historical connection to the Nagari script as attested by the epigraphical evidence.[[275]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDhanesh_JainGeorge_Cardona200770,_75–77_in_Chapter_3_by_Salomon-302)

The Nagari script used for Classical Sanskrit has the fullest repertoire of characters consisting of fourteen vowels and thirty three consonants. For Vedic Sanskrit, it has two more allophonic consonantal characters (the intervocalic ळ *ḷa*, and ळ्ह *ḷha*).[[276]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDhanesh_JainGeorge_Cardona200775–77_in_Chapter_3_by_Salomon-303) To communicate phonetic accuracy, it also includes several modifiers such as the *anusvara* dot and the *visarga* double dot, punctuation symbols and others such as the *halanta* sign.[[276]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDhanesh_JainGeorge_Cardona200775–77_in_Chapter_3_by_Salomon-303)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Phrase_sanskrit.svg/330px-Phrase_sanskrit.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Phrase_sanskrit.svg)

Sanskrit in modern Indian and other Brahmi scripts: *May [Śiva](/wiki/Shiva "Shiva") bless those who take delight in the language of the gods.* ([Kālidāsa](/wiki/K%C4%81lid%C4%81sa "Kālidāsa"))

Other scripts such as [Gujarati](/wiki/Gujarati_alphabet "Gujarati alphabet"), [Bangla-Assamese](/wiki/Bengali-Assamese_script "Bengali-Assamese script"), [Odia](/wiki/Odia_alphabet "Odia alphabet") and major south Indian scripts, states Salomon, "have been and often still are used in their proper territories for writing Sanskrit".[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDhanesh_JainGeorge_Cardona200768–70_in_Chapter_3_by_Salomon-297) These and many Indian scripts look different to the untrained eye, but the differences between Indic scripts is "mostly superficial and they share the same phonetic repertoire and systemic features", states Salomon.[[277]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDhanesh_JainGeorge_Cardona200770–78_in_Chapter_3_by_Salomon-304) They all have essentially the same set of eleven to fourteen vowels and thirty-three consonants as established by the Sanskrit language and attestable in the Brahmi script. Further, a closer examination reveals that they all have the similar basic graphic principles, the same *varnamala* (literally, "garland of letters") alphabetic ordering following the same logical phonetic order, easing the work of historic skilled scribes writing or reproducing Sanskrit works across South Asia.[[278]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDhanesh_JainGeorge_Cardona200770–71,_75–76_in_Chapter_3_by_Salomon-305)[[ab]](#cite_note-307)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Mandakapattu_Inscription.jpg/330px-Mandakapattu_Inscription.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mandakapattu_Inscription.jpg)

One of the earliest known Sanskrit inscriptions in Tamil Grantha script at a rock-cut Hindu Trimurti temple ([Mandakapattu](/wiki/Mandagapattu_Temple "Mandagapattu Temple"), c. 615 CE)

In the south, where [Dravidian languages](/wiki/Dravidian_languages "Dravidian languages") predominate, scripts used for Sanskrit include the [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada_alphabet "Kannada alphabet"), [Telugu](/wiki/Telugu_script "Telugu script"), [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam_script "Malayalam script") and [Grantha alphabets](/wiki/Grantha_alphabet "Grantha alphabet").

### Transliteration and romanisation schemes

Main articles: [Devanagari transliteration](/wiki/Devanagari_transliteration "Devanagari transliteration") and [International Alphabet of Sanskrit Transliteration](/wiki/International_Alphabet_of_Sanskrit_Transliteration "International Alphabet of Sanskrit Transliteration")

Since the late 18th century, Sanskrit has been [transliterated](/wiki/Transliteration "Transliteration") using the [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet"). The system most commonly used today is the IAST ([International Alphabet of Sanskrit Transliteration](/wiki/International_Alphabet_of_Sanskrit_Transliteration "International Alphabet of Sanskrit Transliteration")), which has been the academic standard since 1888. [ASCII](/wiki/ASCII "ASCII")-based transliteration schemes have also evolved because of difficulties representing Sanskrit characters in computer systems. These include [Harvard-Kyoto](/wiki/Harvard-Kyoto "Harvard-Kyoto") and [ITRANS](/wiki/ITRANS "ITRANS"), a transliteration scheme that is used widely on the Internet, especially in Usenet and in email, for considerations of speed of entry as well as rendering issues. With the wide availability of [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode")-aware web browsers, IAST has become common online. It is also possible to type using an [alphanumeric keyboard](/wiki/Alphanumeric_keyboard "Alphanumeric keyboard") and transliterate to Devanagari using software like Mac OS X's international support.

Starting in about the 1st century BCE, Sanskrit has been written in many South Asian, Southeast Asian and Central Asian scripts.

[Sanskrit epigraphy](/wiki/Sanskrit_epigraphy "Sanskrit epigraphy"), the study of ancient inscriptions in Sanskrit, offers insight into the [linguistic](/wiki/Languages_of_South_Asia "Languages of South Asia"), [cultural](/wiki/Culture_of_South_Asia "Culture of South Asia"), and [historical evolution](/wiki/South_Asian_history "South Asian history") of South Asia and its neighbors. [Early inscriptions](/wiki/Early_Indian_inscriptions "Early Indian inscriptions"), such as those from the 1st century [BCE](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era") in [Ayodhya](/wiki/Ayodhya "Ayodhya") and [Hathibada](/wiki/Hathibada "Hathibada"), are written in [Brahmi script](/wiki/Brahmi_script "Brahmi script") and reflect the transition to [classical Sanskrit](/wiki/Classical_Sanskrit "Classical Sanskrit"). The Mathura inscriptions from the 1st and 2nd centuries [CE](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era"), including the [Mora Well](/wiki/Mora_Well_Inscription "Mora Well Inscription") and [Vasu Doorjamb](/wiki/Vasu_Doorjamb_Inscription "Vasu Doorjamb Inscription") inscriptions, represent significant contributions to the early use of Sanskrit, often linked to Hindu and Jaina traditions.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon199886–87-152)[[280]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQuintanilla2007260-308)

Sanskrit inscriptions extended beyond South Asia, influencing Southeast Asia from the 4th century CE onward. [Indic scripts](/wiki/Indic_scripts "Indic scripts") adapted for Sanskrit were found in regions like Vietnam, Malaysia, Indonesia, and Cambodia, where they evolved into local scripts such as Khmer, Javanese, and Balinese. These inscriptions highlight the spread of [Indian cultural](/wiki/Indian_culture "Indian culture") and [religious practices](/wiki/Indian_religion "Indian religion").[[281]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECourt1996445–448-309)[[282]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMasica1993143–144-310)[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon199892–93-311)

## Literature

Main articles: [Sanskrit literature](/wiki/Sanskrit_literature "Sanskrit literature") and [Sanskrit Buddhist literature](/wiki/Sanskrit_Buddhist_literature "Sanskrit Buddhist literature")

Literature in Sanskrit[[ac]](#cite_note-313) can be broadly divided into texts composed in Vedic Sanskrit and the later Classical Sanskrit.[[285]](#cite_note-314) Vedic Sanskrit is the language of the extensive liturgical works of the Vedic religion,[[ad]](#cite_note-316) which aside from the four Vedas, include the Brāhmaṇas and the Sūtras.[[287]](#cite_note-317)[[288]](#cite_note-318)[[289]](#cite_note-319)

The Vedic literature that survives is entirely of a religious form, whereas works in Classical Sanskrit exist in a wide variety of fields including epics, lyric, drama, romance, fairytale, fables, grammar, civil and religious law, the science of politics and practical life, the science of love and sex, philosophy, medicine, astronomy, astrology and mathematics, and is largely secular in subject-matter.[[290]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIyengar1965xxx–xxxiii,_5-320)[[291]](#cite_note-321)

While Vedic literature is essentially optimistic in spirit, portraying man as strong and powerful capable of finding fulfilment both here and in the afterworld, the later literature is pessimistic, portraying humans as controlled by the forces of fate with worldly pleasures deemed the cause of misery. These fundamental differences in psychology are attributed to the absence of the doctrines of Karma and reincarnation in the Vedic period, notions which are very prevalent in later times.[[292]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIyengar19655-322)

| Design, architecture (Vastu, Śilpa) | Śilpa·śāstra | [[321]](#cite_note-354)[[322]](#cite_note-355) |
| Temples, Sculpture | Bṛhat·saṃhitā | [[323]](#cite_note-356) |
| Saṃskāra (rites-of-passage) | Gṛhya·sūtras | [[324]](#cite_note-357) |
| Buddhism | Sutras, Vinaya, Kāvya, Medicine, [Buddhist philosophy](/wiki/Buddhist_philosophy "Buddhist philosophy") | [Tripiṭaka](/wiki/Tripitaka "Tripitaka"),[[ah]](#cite_note-359) [Mahayana sutras](/wiki/Mahayana_sutras "Mahayana sutras") and [shastras](/wiki/Shastra "Shastra"), [tantras](/wiki/Tantras_(Buddhism) "Tantras (Buddhism)"), grammar texts, [Buddhist poetry](/wiki/Buddhist_poetry "Buddhist poetry"), drama, Buddhist medical texts | [[325]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBanerji1989634–635_with_the_list_in_Appendix_IX-358)[[326]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEltschinger2017-360)[[327]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWayman1965-361) |
| Jainism | Theology, philosophy | [Tattvārtha Sūtra](/wiki/Tattvartha_Sutra "Tattvartha Sutra"), Mahāpurāṇa and others | [[328]](#cite_note-362)[[329]](#cite_note-363) |

## Lexicon

See also: [Indo-European vocabulary](/wiki/Indo-European_vocabulary "Indo-European vocabulary") and [Sanskrit compound](/wiki/Sanskrit_compound "Sanskrit compound")

As an Indo-European language, Sanskrit's core [lexicon](/wiki/Lexicon "Lexicon") is inherited from Proto-Indo-European. Over time however, the language exhibits a tendency to shed many of these inherited words and borrow others in their place from other sources.

In the oldest Vedic literature, there are few such non-Indo-European words, but these progressively grow in volume.[[330]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurrow2001375-364)

The following are some of the old Indo-European words that eventually fade out of use in Sanskrit:[[331]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurrow200139–40-365)

:   |  |  |  |
    | --- | --- | --- |
    | *ápas* | work | cf. Latin *opus* |
    | *kravís* | raw flesh | cf. Latin *crūdus* |
    | *dáma-* | house | cf. Latin *domus* |
    | *dā́nu-* | moisture |  |
    | *háras-* | heat |  |

### Dravidian lexical influence

The sources of these new loanwords are many, and vary across the different regions of the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"). But of all influences on the lexicon of Sanskrit, the most important is Dravidian.

The following is a list of Dravidian entrants into Sanskrit lexicon, although some may have been contested:[[332]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurrow2001381–385-366)[[333]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParpola1994168-367)

:   |  |  |  |
    | --- | --- | --- |
    | *phálam* | ripe fruit | Proto-Dravidian *\*paẓam* |
    | *múkham* | mouth | Proto-Dravidian *\*mukam* |
    | *kajjala-* | soot, lampblack |  |
    | *kaṭu-* | sharp, pungent |  |
    | *kaṭhina-* | hard, firm |  |
    | *kuṭi-* | hut, house |  |
    | *kuṭṭ-* | to pound |  |
    | *kuṇḍala-* | loop, ring, earring,  coil of rope |  |
    | *khala-* | a rogue |  |
    | *mayū́ra-* | peacock |  |
    | *mallikā* | jasmine |  |
    | *mīna-* | fish |  |
    | *vallī-* | creeper |  |
    | *heramba-* | buffalo |  |

### Nominal-form preference

While Vedic and epic form of speech is largely cognate to that of other [Indo-European languages](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages") such as Greek and Latin, later Sanskrit shows a tendency to move away from using verbal forms to nominal ones. Examples of nominal forms taking the place of conventional conjugation are:

:   |  |  |  |
    | --- | --- | --- |
    | past passive participle | *naraḥ gataḥ* | "the man went",  (lit. 'The man was gone') |
    | active past participle in *-vant* | *kṛtavān* | "he had done" (lit. 'He, having done, (is)') |

However the most notable development is the prolific use of word-compounding to express ideas normally conveyed by verbal forms and sub-clauses introduced by conjunctions.[[334]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeith199620–21-368)

Classical Sanskrit's pre-eminent playwright [Kālidāsa](/wiki/K%C4%81lid%C4%81sa "Kālidāsa") uses:

:   |  |  |
    | --- | --- |
    | *vīcikṣobhastanitavihagaśreṇikāñcīguṇā* | whose girdle-string is a row of birds,  loquacious through the agitation of the waves |

## Influence on other languages

See also: [Sanskritisation (language)](/wiki/Sanskritisation_(language) "Sanskritisation (language)"), [Indosphere](/wiki/Indosphere "Indosphere"), and [Greater India](/wiki/Greater_India "Greater India")

For nearly 2,000 years, Sanskrit was the language of a cultural order that exerted influence across South Asia, [Inner Asia](/wiki/Inner_Asia "Inner Asia"), Southeast Asia, and to a certain extent East Asia.[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPollock2001-185) A significant form of post-Vedic Sanskrit is found in the Sanskrit of [Indian epic poetry](/wiki/Indian_epic_poetry "Indian epic poetry")—the *[Ramayana](/wiki/Ramayana "Ramayana")* and *[Mahabharata](/wiki/Mahabharata "Mahabharata")*. The deviations from *[Pāṇini](/wiki/P%C4%81%E1%B9%87ini "Pāṇini")* in the epics are generally considered to be on account of interference from [Prakrits](/wiki/Prakrit "Prakrit"), or innovations, and not because they are pre-Paninian.[[335]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOberlies2003xxvii–xxix-369) Traditional Sanskrit scholars call such deviations *ārṣa* (आर्ष), meaning 'of the [ṛṣis](/wiki/Rishi "Rishi")', the traditional title for the ancient authors. In some contexts, there are also more "prakritisms" (borrowings from common speech) than in Classical Sanskrit proper. [Buddhist Hybrid Sanskrit](/wiki/Buddhist_Hybrid_Sanskrit "Buddhist Hybrid Sanskrit") is a literary language heavily influenced by the [Middle Indo-Aryan languages](/wiki/Middle_Indo-Aryan_languages "Middle Indo-Aryan languages"), based on early Buddhist Prakrit texts which subsequently assimilated to the Classical Sanskrit standard in varying degrees.[[336]](#cite_note-Edgerton_2004-370)

### Indian subcontinent

Sanskrit has greatly influenced the languages of India that grew from its vocabulary and grammatical base; for instance, [Hindi](/wiki/Standard_Hindi "Standard Hindi") is a "Sanskritised register" of [Hindustani](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language"). All modern [Indo-Aryan languages](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages"), as well as [Munda](/wiki/Munda_languages "Munda languages") and [Dravidian languages](/wiki/Dravidian_languages "Dravidian languages") have borrowed many words either directly from Sanskrit (*[tatsama](/wiki/Tatsama "Tatsama")* words), or indirectly via middle Indo-Aryan languages (*[tadbhava](/wiki/Tadbhava "Tadbhava")* words). Words originating in Sanskrit are estimated at fifty percent of the vocabulary of modern Indo-Aryan languages, as well as the literary forms of [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam") and [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada "Kannada").[[337]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStaal1963261-371) Literary texts in [Telugu](/wiki/Telugu_language "Telugu language") are [lexically](/wiki/Lexicon "Lexicon") Sanskrit or Sanskritised to an enormous extent, perhaps seventy percent or more.[[338]](#cite_note-Rao_2002_p.-372) [Marathi](/wiki/Marathi_language "Marathi language") is another prominent language in Western India, that derives most of its words and [Marathi grammar](/wiki/Marathi_grammar "Marathi grammar") from Sanskrit.[[339]](#cite_note-373) Sanskrit words are often preferred in the literary texts in Marathi over corresponding colloquial Marathi word.[[340]](#cite_note-374)

There has been a profound influence of Sanskrit on the lexical and grammatical systems of Dravidian languages. As per Dalby, India has been a single cultural area for about two millennia which has helped Sanskrit influence on all the Indic languages.[[341]](#cite_note-:0-375) Emeneau and Burrow mention the tendency "for all four of the Dravidian literary languages in South to make literary use of total Sanskrit lexicon indiscriminately".[[342]](#cite_note-376) There are a large number of loanwords found in the vocabulary of the three major Dravidian languages Malayalam, Kannada and Telugu.[[341]](#cite_note-:0-375) Tamil also has significant loanwords from Sanskrit.[[343]](#cite_note-:3-377) Krishnamurthi mentions that although it is not clear when the Sanskrit influence happened on the Dravidian languages, it might have been around the 5th century BCE at the time of separation of Tamil and Kannada from a [common ancestral stage](/wiki/Tamil%E2%80%93Kannada_languages "Tamil–Kannada languages").[[344]](#cite_note-378) ‌The borrowed words are classified into two types based on phonological integration – *tadbhava* – those words derived from Prakrit and *tatsama* – unassimilated loanwords from Sanskrit.[[345]](#cite_note-:1-379)

Strazny mentions that "so massive has been the influence that it is hard to utter Sanskrit words have influenced Kannada from the early times".[[346]](#cite_note-380) The first document in Kannada, the Halmidi inscription has a large number of Sanskrit words. As per Kachru, the influence has not only been on single lexical items in Kannada but also on "long nominal compounds and complicated syntactic expressions". New words have been created in Kannada using Sanskrit derivational prefixes and suffixes like *vikēndrīkaraṇa, anilīkaraṇa, bahīskruṭa*. Similar stratification is found in verb morphology. Sanskrit words readily undergo verbalization in Kannada, verbalizing suffixes as in: *chāpisu, dauḍāyisu, ravānisu.*[[347]](#cite_note-381)

George mentions that "No other Dravidian language has been so deeply influenced by Sanskrit as Malayalam".[[348]](#cite_note-382) According to Lambert, Malayalam is so immensely Sanskritised that every Sanskrit word can be used in Malayalam by integrating "prosodic phonological" changes as per Grant.[[349]](#cite_note-383) Loanwords have been integrated into Malayalam by "prosodic phonological" changes as per Grant. These phonological changes are either by replacement of a vowel as in *sant-*am coming from Sanskrit *santa*, *sāgar*-am from *sāgara*, or addition of prothetic vowel as in *aracan* from *rājā-*, *uruvam* from *rūpa*, *codyam* from *sodhya*.[[345]](#cite_note-:1-379)

Hans Henrich et al. note that, the language of the pre-modern Telugu literature was also highly influenced by Sanskrit and was standardized between 11th and 14th centuries.[[350]](#cite_note-:2-384) Aiyar has shown that in a class of *tadbhavas* in Telugu the first and second letters are often replaced by the third and fourth letters and fourth again replaced often by h. Examples of the same are: Sanskrit *artha* becomes *ardhama*, *vīthi* becomes *vidhi*, *putra* becomes *bidda*, *mukham* becomes *muhamu*.[[351]](#cite_note-385)

[Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language") also has been influenced by Sanskrit. Hans Henrich et al. mention that propagation of Jainism and Buddhism into south India had its influence.[[350]](#cite_note-:2-384) Shulman mentions that although contrary to the views held by Tamil purists, modern Tamil has been significantly influenced from Sanskrit, further states that "Indeed, there may well be more Sanskrit in Tamil than in the Sanskrit derived north-Indian vernaculars". Sanskrit words have been Tamilized through the "Tamil phonematic grid".[[343]](#cite_note-:3-377)

### Beyond the Indian subcontinent

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/A_Sanskrit_manuscript_of_Lotus_Sutra_in_South_Turkestan_Brahmi_script.jpg/330px-A_Sanskrit_manuscript_of_Lotus_Sutra_in_South_Turkestan_Brahmi_script.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_Sanskrit_manuscript_of_Lotus_Sutra_in_South_Turkestan_Brahmi_script.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/A_palm_leaf_Sanskrit_manuscript_in_Brahmi_script_from_Miran_China.jpg/330px-A_palm_leaf_Sanskrit_manuscript_in_Brahmi_script_from_Miran_China.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_palm_leaf_Sanskrit_manuscript_in_Brahmi_script_from_Miran_China.jpg)

Sanskrit has had a historical presence and influence in many parts of Asia. Above (top clockwise): [i] a Sanskrit manuscript from Turkestan, [ii] another from Miran-China.

Sanskrit was a language for religious purposes and for the political elite in parts of medieval era Southeast Asia, Central Asia and East Asia, having been introduced in these regions mainly along with the spread of Buddhism. In some cases, it has competed with Pāli for prominence.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMichael_C._Howard201221-174)[[352]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurrow200163-386)

#### East Asia

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/South_Korea-Goheunggun-Neunggasa_5836-07_bronze_bell.JPG/250px-South_Korea-Goheunggun-Neunggasa_5836-07_bronze_bell.JPG)](/wiki/File:South_Korea-Goheunggun-Neunggasa_5836-07_bronze_bell.JPG)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Siddham_alphabet_by_Kukai.svg/120px-Siddham_alphabet_by_Kukai.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Siddham_alphabet_by_Kukai.svg)

[i] a bell with Sanskrit engravings in South Korea [ii] the [Kūkai](/wiki/K%C5%ABkai "Kūkai") calligraphy of Siddham-Sanskrit in Japan

[Buddhist Sanskrit](/wiki/Buddhist_Hybrid_Sanskrit "Buddhist Hybrid Sanskrit") has had a considerable influence on [Sino-Tibetan languages](/wiki/Sino-Tibetan_languages "Sino-Tibetan languages") such as Chinese, state William Wang and Chaofen Sun.[[353]](#cite_note-WangSun2015p5-387) Many words have been adopted from Sanskrit into Chinese, both in its historic religious discourse and everyday use.[[353]](#cite_note-WangSun2015p5-387)[[ai]](#cite_note-388) This process likely started about 200 CE and continued through about 1400 CE, with the efforts of monks such as Yuezhi, Anxi, Kangju, Tianzhu, Yan Fodiao, Faxian, Xuanzang and [Yijing](/wiki/Yijing_(monk) "Yijing (monk)").[[353]](#cite_note-WangSun2015p5-387)

Further, as the Chinese languages and culture influenced the rest of East Asia, the ideas in Sanskrit texts and some of its linguistic elements migrated further.[[158]](#cite_note-OrzechSørensen2011-172)[[354]](#cite_note-389)

Many terms were transliterated directly and added to the Chinese vocabulary. Chinese words like 剎那 *chànà* ([Devanagari](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari"): क्षण *kṣaṇa* 'instantaneous period') were borrowed from Sanskrit. Many Sanskrit texts survive only in Tibetan collections of commentaries to the Buddhist teachings, the [Tengyur](/wiki/Tengyur "Tengyur").

Sanskrit has also influenced the religious register of Japanese mostly through transliterations. These were borrowed from Chinese transliterations.[[355]](#cite_note-390) In particular, the [Shingon](/wiki/Shingon "Shingon") (lit. 'True Words') sect of esoteric Buddhism has been relying on Sanskrit and original Sanskrit [mantras](/wiki/Mantra "Mantra") and writings, as a means of realizing Buddhahood.[[356]](#cite_note-391)

#### Southeast Asia

Further information: [Sanskrit inscriptions in the Malay world](/wiki/Sanskrit_inscriptions_in_the_Malay_world "Sanskrit inscriptions in the Malay world")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Sanskrit_n_Thai.jpg/250px-Sanskrit_n_Thai.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sanskrit_n_Thai.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Cambodia-2742_%283621496153%29.jpg/120px-Cambodia-2742_%283621496153%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cambodia-2742_(3621496153).jpg)

[i] the Thai script [ii] a Sanskrit inscription in Cambodia

A large number of inscriptions in Sanskrit across Southeast Asia testify the influence the language held in these regions.[[357]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurrow200163,_64-392)

Languages such as [Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesia_language "Indonesia language"), [Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language") and [Lao](/wiki/Lao_language "Lao language") contain many [loanwords](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword") from Sanskrit, as does [Khmer](/wiki/Khmer_language "Khmer language"). Many Sanskrit loanwords are also found in [Austronesian languages](/wiki/Austronesian_languages "Austronesian languages"), such as [Javanese](/wiki/Javanese_language "Javanese language"), particularly the [older form](/wiki/Old_Javanese "Old Javanese") in which nearly half the vocabulary is borrowed.[[358]](#cite_note-393)

Other Austronesian languages, such as [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language") (descended into modern [Malaysian](/wiki/Malaysian_language "Malaysian language") and [Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language") standards) also derive [much of their vocabulary](/wiki/List_of_loanwords_in_Indonesian#From_Sanskrit "List of loanwords in Indonesian") from Sanskrit. Similarly, [Philippine languages](/wiki/Philippine_languages "Philippine languages") such as [Tagalog](/wiki/Tagalog_language "Tagalog language") have [some Sanskrit loanwords](/wiki/List_of_loanwords_in_Tagalog#Sanskrit "List of loanwords in Tagalog").

A Sanskrit loanword encountered in many Southeast Asian languages is the word *[bhāṣā](/wiki/Bahasa "Bahasa")*, or spoken language, which is used to refer to the names of many languages.[[359]](#cite_note-394)

To this day, Southeast Asian languages such as Thai are known to draw upon Sanskrit for technical vocabulary.[[360]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurrow200164-395)

##### Indonesia

Further information: [List of Indic loanwords in Indonesian](/wiki/List_of_Indic_loanwords_in_Indonesian "List of Indic loanwords in Indonesian")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/33/Kutai_Prasasti_of_Mulawarman.JPG)](/wiki/File:Kutai_Prasasti_of_Mulawarman.JPG)

The ancient [Yūpa](/wiki/Y%C5%ABpa#Yūpa_inscription_in_Indonesia "Yūpa") inscription (one of the earliest and oldest Sanskrit texts written in ancient Indonesia) dating back to the [4th century CE](/wiki/4th_century_CE "4th century CE") written by [Brahmins](/wiki/Brahmin "Brahmin") under the rule of King [Mulavarman](/wiki/Mulavarman "Mulavarman") of the [Kutai Martadipura Kingdom](/wiki/Kutai#History "Kutai") located in [eastern Borneo](/wiki/East_Kalimantan "East Kalimantan")

The earliest Sanskrit text which was founded in the [Indonesian Archipelago](/wiki/Indonesian_Archipelago "Indonesian Archipelago") was at [Eastern Borneo](/wiki/East_Kalimantan "East Kalimantan") dating back to 400 CE known as the [Mulavarman inscription](/wiki/Y%C5%ABpa#Yūpa_inscription_in_Indonesia "Yūpa").[[361]](#cite_note-396) This is one of the reason of strong influence of Indian culture that entered the [Malay Archipelago](/wiki/Malay_Archipelago "Malay Archipelago") during the [Indianization era](/wiki/Greater_India "Greater India"), and since then, Indian culture has been absorbed towards Indonesian culture and language. Thus, the Sanskrit culture in Indonesia exists not as a religious aspect but more towards a cultural aspect that has been present for generations, resulting in a more cultural rather than Hinduistic value of the [Indonesian people](/wiki/Indonesian_people "Indonesian people"). As a result, it is common to find Muslim or Christian Indonesians with names that have Indian or Sanskrit nuances. Unlike names derived from Sanskrit in [Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language") and [Khmer](/wiki/Khmer_language "Khmer language"), the pronunciation of Sanskrit names in Indonesia is more similar to the original Indian pronunciation, except that "v" is changed to "w", for example, "Vishnu" in India will be spelled "Wisnu" in Indonesia.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Rest of the world

In ancient and medieval times, several Sanskrit words in the field of food and spices made their way into European languages including Greek, Latin and later English. Some of these are *pepper*, *ginger* and *sugar*. English today has several [words of Sanskrit origin](/wiki/List_of_English_words_of_Sanskrit_origin "List of English words of Sanskrit origin"), most of them borrowed during the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") or later. Some of these words have in turn been borrowed by other European or world languages.[[362]](#cite_note-397)

## Modern era

### Liturgy, ceremonies and meditation

Sanskrit is the sacred language of various Hindu, Buddhist, and [Jain](/wiki/Jain "Jain") traditions. It is used during worship in [Hindu temples](/wiki/Hindu_temple "Hindu temple"). In [Newar Buddhism](/wiki/Newar_Buddhism "Newar Buddhism"), it is used in all monasteries. Sanskrit mantras and Sanskrit as a ritual language was commonplace among Jains throughout their medieval history.[[363]](#cite_note-398)

Many Hindu rituals and rites-of-passage such as the "giving away the bride" and mutual vows at weddings, a baby's naming or first solid food ceremony and the goodbye during a cremation invoke and chant Sanskrit hymns.[[364]](#cite_note-399) Major festivals such as the *[Durga Puja](/wiki/Durga_Puja "Durga Puja")* ritually recite entire Sanskrit texts such as the *[Devi Mahatmya](/wiki/Devi_Mahatmya "Devi Mahatmya")* every year particularly among the numerous communities of eastern India.[[365]](#cite_note-400)[[366]](#cite_note-401) In the south, Sanskrit texts are recited at many major Hindu temples such as the [Meenakshi Temple](/wiki/Meenakshi_Temple "Meenakshi Temple").[[367]](#cite_note-402) In India and beyond, recitations of the *[Bhagavad Gita](/wiki/Bhagavad_Gita "Bhagavad Gita")* occur in diverse settings, including "simple private household readings, to family and neighborhood recitation sessions, to holy men reciting in temples or at pilgrimage places for passersby, to public Gita discourses held almost nightly at halls and auditoriums in every Indian city".[[368]](#cite_note-403)

### Literature and arts

See also: [List of Sahitya Akademi Award winners for Sanskrit](/wiki/List_of_Sahitya_Akademi_Award_winners_for_Sanskrit "List of Sahitya Akademi Award winners for Sanskrit")

More than 3,000 Sanskrit works have been composed since India's independence in 1947.[[369]](#cite_note-404) Much of this work has been judged of high quality, in comparison to both classical Sanskrit literature and modern literature in other Indian languages.[[370]](#cite_note-405)[[371]](#cite_note-406) In 2009, [Satya Vrat Shastri](/wiki/Satya_Vrat_Shastri "Satya Vrat Shastri") became the first Sanskrit author to win the [Jnanpith Award](/wiki/Jnanpith_Award "Jnanpith Award"), India's highest literary award.[[372]](#cite_note-407)

Sanskrit is used extensively in the [Carnatic](/wiki/Carnatic_music "Carnatic music") and [Hindustani](/wiki/Hindustani_classical_music "Hindustani classical music") branches of classical music. [Kirtanas](/wiki/Kirtan "Kirtan"), [bhajans](/wiki/Bhajan "Bhajan"), [stotras](/wiki/Stotra "Stotra"), and [shlokas](/wiki/Shloka "Shloka") of Sanskrit are popular throughout India. The [Samaveda](/wiki/Samaveda "Samaveda") uses musical notations in several of its recessions.[[373]](#cite_note-brit_samveda-408)

In [Mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China"), musicians such as [Sa Dingding](/wiki/Sa_Dingding "Sa Dingding") have written [pop songs](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") in Sanskrit.[[374]](#cite_note-409)

Numerous loan Sanskrit words are found in other major Asian languages. For example, [Filipino](/wiki/List_of_loanwords_in_Tagalog#Sanskrit "List of loanwords in Tagalog"),[[375]](#cite_note-410) [Cebuano](/wiki/Cebuano_language "Cebuano language"),[[376]](#cite_note-kuizon-411) [Lao](/wiki/Lao_language "Lao language"), [Khmer](/wiki/Khmer_language "Khmer language"),[[377]](#cite_note-Sak-412) [Thai](/wiki/List_of_loanwords_in_Thai "List of loanwords in Thai") and its [alphabets](/wiki/Thai_alphabet#Sanskrit_and_Pali "Thai alphabet"), [Malay](/wiki/List_of_loanwords_in_Malay "List of loanwords in Malay") (including [Malaysian](/wiki/Malaysian_language "Malaysian language") and [Indonesian](/wiki/List_of_loanwords_in_Indonesian#From_Sanskrit "List of loanwords in Indonesian")), [Javanese](/wiki/Javanese_language "Javanese language") (old Javanese-English dictionary by [P.J. Zoetmulder](/wiki/P.J._Zoetmulder "P.J. Zoetmulder") contains over 25,500 entries), and even in [English](/wiki/List_of_English_words_of_Sanskrit_origin "List of English words of Sanskrit origin").

### Media

Since 1974, there has been a short daily news broadcast on [All India Radio](/wiki/All_India_Radio "All India Radio").[[378]](#cite_note-Mint2012-413) These broadcasts are also made available on the internet on AIR's website.[[379]](#cite_note-414)[[380]](#cite_note-415) Sanskrit news is broadcast on TV and on the internet through the DD National channel.[[381]](#cite_note-416) Over 90 weeklies, fortnightlies and quarterlies are published in Sanskrit. *[Sudharma](/wiki/Sudharma "Sudharma")*, a daily printed newspaper in Sanskrit, has been published out of [Mysore](/wiki/Mysore "Mysore"), India, since 1970. It was started by K.N. Varadaraja Iyengar, a Sanskrit scholar from [Mysore](/wiki/Mysore "Mysore").[[382]](#cite_note-417)

### Schools and contemporary status

See also: [Sanskrit revival](/wiki/Sanskrit_revival "Sanskrit revival")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Sanskrit_festival_at_Pramati_School%2C_Mysore.jpg/250px-Sanskrit_festival_at_Pramati_School%2C_Mysore.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sanskrit_festival_at_Pramati_School,_Mysore.jpg)

Sanskrit festival at [Pramati Hillview Academy](/wiki/Pramati_Hillview_Academy "Pramati Hillview Academy"), [Mysore](/wiki/Mysore "Mysore"), India

Sanskrit has been taught in schools from time immemorial in India. In modern times, the first Sanskrit University was [Sampurnanand Sanskrit University](/wiki/Sampurnanand_Sanskrit_University "Sampurnanand Sanskrit University"), established in 1791 in the Indian city of [Varanasi](/wiki/Varanasi "Varanasi"). Sanskrit is taught in 5,000 traditional schools (Pathashalas), and 14,000 schools[[383]](#cite_note-418) in India. Sanskrit is one of the 22 [scheduled languages of India](/wiki/Languages_with_official_status_in_India#Scheduled_languages_of_the_Indian_Constitution "Languages with official status in India").[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaniels1996371–372-285) Despite it being a studied school subject in contemporary India, Sanskrit has not been spoken as a native language in centuries.[[6]](#cite_note-patrick-mccartney-5-10-20-6)[[7]](#cite_note-patrick-mccartney-5-11-20-7)[[8]](#cite_note-sreevastan-thehindu-sanskrit-8)

In India, Sanskrit is offered as a language in central and several state education board schools and is also taught in traditional [gurukulas](/wiki/Gurukula "Gurukula") across the country.[[384]](#cite_note-419) A number of [colleges and universities](/wiki/List_of_Sanskrit_universities_in_India "List of Sanskrit universities in India") in India have dedicated departments for Sanskrit studies. In March 2020, the [Indian Parliament](/wiki/Indian_Parliament "Indian Parliament") passed the *Central Sanskrit Universities Act, 2020* which upgraded three universities, [National Sanskrit University](/wiki/National_Sanskrit_University "National Sanskrit University"), [Central Sanskrit University](/wiki/Central_Sanskrit_University "Central Sanskrit University") and [Shri Lal Bahadur Shastri National Sanskrit University](/wiki/Shri_Lal_Bahadur_Shastri_National_Sanskrit_University "Shri Lal Bahadur Shastri National Sanskrit University"), from the [deemed to be university](/wiki/Deemed_to_be_university "Deemed to be university") status to a [central university](/wiki/Central_university_(India) "Central university (India)") status.[[385]](#cite_note-420)

[Dmitri Mendeleev](/wiki/Dmitri_Mendeleev "Dmitri Mendeleev") used the Sanskrit numbers of one, two and three (*eka-*, *dvi-* or *dwi-*, and *tri-* respectively) to give provisional names to his predicted elements, like eka-boron being [Gallium](/wiki/Gallium "Gallium") or eka-[Francium](/wiki/Francium "Francium") being [Ununennium](/wiki/Ununennium "Ununennium").

In the province of [Bali](/wiki/Bali "Bali") in Indonesia, a number of educational and scholarly institutions have also been conducting Sanskrit lessons for Hindu locals.[[386]](#cite_note-421)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

#### In the West

See also: [Sanskrit revival § Revival outside India](/wiki/Sanskrit_revival#Revival_outside_India "Sanskrit revival")

[St James Junior School](/wiki/St_James_Independent_Schools "St James Independent Schools") and [Avanti Schools Trust](/wiki/Avanti_Schools_Trust "Avanti Schools Trust") in London, England, offer Sanskrit as part of the curriculum.[[387]](#cite_note-422) Since September 2009, US high school students have been able to receive credits as Independent Study or toward Foreign Language requirements by studying Sanskrit as part of the "SAFL: Samskritam as a Foreign Language" program coordinated by [Samskrita Bharati](/wiki/Samskrita_Bharati "Samskrita Bharati").[[388]](#cite_note-423) In Australia, the private boys' high school [Sydney Grammar School](/wiki/Sydney_Grammar_School "Sydney Grammar School") offers Sanskrit from years 7 through to 12, including for the [Higher School Certificate](/wiki/Higher_School_Certificate_(New_South_Wales) "Higher School Certificate (New South Wales)").[[389]](#cite_note-424) Other schools that offer Sanskrit include the Ficino School in Auckland, New Zealand; St James Preparatory Schools in Cape Town, Durban and Johannesburg, South Africa; John Colet School, Sydney, Australia; Erasmus School, Melbourne, Australia.[[390]](#cite_note-425)[[391]](#cite_note-426)[[392]](#cite_note-427)

### European studies and discourse

See also: [Sanskrit studies](/wiki/Sanskrit_studies "Sanskrit studies")

While European scholarship in Sanskrit, begun by [Heinrich Roth](/wiki/Heinrich_Roth "Heinrich Roth") (1620–1668) and [Johann Ernst Hanxleden](/wiki/Johann_Ernst_Hanxleden "Johann Ernst Hanxleden") (1681–1731), is considered responsible for the discovery of an Indo-European language family by [Sir William Jones](/wiki/William_Jones_(philologist) "William Jones (philologist)") (1746–1794) (with this research playing an important role in the development of Western [philology](/wiki/Philology "Philology"), or historical linguistics),[[393]](#cite_note-Müller1859-428) the first scholar to suggest this was the Delhi-based [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") scholar [Siraj-ud-Din Ali Khan Arzu](/wiki/Siraj-ud-Din_Ali_Khan_Arzu "Siraj-ud-Din Ali Khan Arzu") (1687-1756) in his Persian-language [philological](/wiki/Philology "Philology") treatise *Muzmir* ("Fruitful").[[394]](#cite_note-Truschke2025_p19-429)[[395]](#cite_note-Musmir-430)

The 18th- and 19th-century speculations about the possible links of Sanskrit to ancient Egyptian language were later proven to be wrong, but it fed an orientalist discourse both in the form Indophobia and Indophilia, states Trautmann.[[396]](#cite_note-431) Sanskrit writings, when first discovered, were imagined by Indophiles to potentially be "repositories of the primitive experiences and religion of the human race, and as such confirmatory of the truth of Christian scripture", as well as a key to "universal ethnological narrative".[[397]](#cite_note-Tautmann-2004-432): 96–97 The Indophobes imagined the opposite, making the counterclaim that there is little of any value in Sanskrit, portraying it as "a language fabricated by artful [Brahmin] priests", with little original thought, possibly copied from the Greeks who came with Alexander or perhaps the Persians.[[397]](#cite_note-Tautmann-2004-432): 124–126

Scholars such as William Jones and his colleagues felt the need for systematic studies of Sanskrit language and literature. This launched [the Asiatic Society](/wiki/The_Asiatic_Society "The Asiatic Society"), an idea that was soon transplanted to Europe starting with the efforts of [Henry Thomas Colebrooke](/wiki/Henry_Thomas_Colebrooke "Henry Thomas Colebrooke") in Britain, then [Alexander Hamilton](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton_(linguist) "Alexander Hamilton (linguist)") who helped expand its studies to Paris and thereafter his student Friedrich Schlegel who introduced Sanskrit to the universities of Germany. Schlegel nurtured his own students into influential European Sanskrit scholars, particularly through [Franz Bopp](/wiki/Franz_Bopp "Franz Bopp") and [Friedrich Max Müller](/wiki/Max_M%C3%BCller "Max Müller"). As these scholars translated the Sanskrit manuscripts, the enthusiasm for Sanskrit grew rapidly among European scholars, states Trautmann, and chairs for Sanskrit "were established in the universities of nearly every German statelet" creating a competition for Sanskrit experts.[[397]](#cite_note-Tautmann-2004-432): 133–142

### Symbolic usage

See also: [Sanskrit honorifics in Southeast Asia](/wiki/Indian_honorifics#Influence_on_other_cultures "Indian honorifics"), [Sanskritised naming of people across the world](/wiki/Indian_name#Global_Indian_influence_in_names "Indian name"), and [Sanskritised naming of places across the world](/wiki/Place_names_in_India#Global_Indian_influence_in_place_name "Place names in India")

In India, [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), Nepal, [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), and Southeast Asia, Sanskrit phrases are widely used as [mottoes](/wiki/Motto "Motto") for various national, educational and social organisations:

* India: *[Satyameva Jayate](/wiki/Satyameva_Jayate "Satyameva Jayate")* (सत्यमेव जयते), meaning 'truth alone triumphs'.[[398]](#cite_note-433)
* [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal"): *[Janani Janmabhūmischa Swargādapi Garīyasī](/wiki/Janani_Janmabhumishcha_Swargadapi_Gariyasi "Janani Janmabhumishcha Swargadapi Gariyasi")* (जननी जन्मभूमिश्च स्वर्गादपि गरीयसी), meaning 'mother and motherland are superior to heaven'.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"): In Indonesia, Sanskrit is widely used as terms and mottoes of the armed forces and other national organizations *(See: [Indonesian Armed Forces mottoes](/wiki/List_of_military_unit_mottoes_by_country#Indonesia "List of military unit mottoes by country"))*. *Rastra Sewakottama* (राष्ट्र सेवकोत्तम, transl. 'people's main servants') is the official motto of the [Indonesian National Police](/wiki/Indonesian_National_Police "Indonesian National Police"), *Tri Dharma Eka Karma* (त्रिधर्म एक कर्म) is the official motto of the [Indonesian Military](/wiki/Indonesian_Military "Indonesian Military"), *Kartika Eka Paksi* (कार्तिक एक पक्षी, transl. 'unmatchable bird with noble goals') is the official motto of the [Indonesian Army](/wiki/Indonesian_Army "Indonesian Army"),[[399]](#cite_note-434) *Adhitakarya Mahatvavirya Nagarabhakti* (अधीतकार्य महत्ववीर्य नगरभक्ति, transl. 'hard-working knights serving bravery as nations hero') is the official motto of the [Indonesian Military Academy](/wiki/Indonesian_Military_Academy "Indonesian Military Academy"),[[400]](#cite_note-435) *Upakriya Labdha Prayojana Balottama* (उपक्रिया लब्ध प्रयोजन बालोत्तम, transl. 'purpose of the unit is to give the best service to the nation by finding the perfect soldier') is the official motto of the Army Psychological Corps, *Karmanye Vadikaraste Mafalesu Kadatjana* (कर्मण्येवाधिकारस्ते मा फलेषु कदाचन, transl. 'working without counting the profit and loss') is the official motto of the Air-Force Special Forces ([Paskhas](/wiki/Paskhas "Paskhas")),[[401]](#cite_note-436) *Jalesu Bhumyamca Jayamahe* (जलेषु भूम्यां च जयामहे, transl. 'on the sea and land we are glorious') is the official motto of the [Indonesian Marine Corps](/wiki/Indonesian_Marine_Corps "Indonesian Marine Corps"),[[402]](#cite_note-437) and there are more units and organizations in Indonesia either Armed Forces or civil which use the Sanskrit language respectively as their mottoes and other purposes.
* Many of India's and Nepal's scientific and administrative terms use Sanskrit. The [Indian guided missile program](/wiki/Integrated_Guided_Missile_Development_Program "Integrated Guided Missile Development Program") that was commenced in 1983 by the [Defence Research and Development Organisation](/wiki/Defence_Research_and_Development_Organisation "Defence Research and Development Organisation") has named the five missiles (ballistic and others) that it developed [Prithvi](/wiki/Prithvi_(missile) "Prithvi (missile)"), [Agni](/wiki/Agni_(missile) "Agni (missile)"), [Akash](/wiki/Akash_(missile) "Akash (missile)"), [Nag](/wiki/Nag_(missile) "Nag (missile)") and the [Trishul missile system](/wiki/Integrated_Guided_Missile_Development_Program#Trishul_missile_system "Integrated Guided Missile Development Program"). India's first modern fighter aircraft is named [HAL Tejas](/wiki/HAL_Tejas "HAL Tejas").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In November 2020, [Gaurav Sharma](/wiki/Gaurav_Sharma_(politician) "Gaurav Sharma (politician)"), a New Zealand politician of Indian origin swore into [parliament](/wiki/New_Zealand_Parliament "New Zealand Parliament") using Sanskrit alongside [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language"); the decision was made as a "homage to all Indian languages" compromising between his native [Pahari](/wiki/Pahari_language "Pahari language") and [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language").[[403]](#cite_note-438)

### In popular culture

The song *[My Sweet Lord](/wiki/My_Sweet_Lord "My Sweet Lord")* by [George Harrison](/wiki/George_Harrison "George Harrison") includes The Hare Krishna mantra, also referred to reverentially as the Maha Mantra, a 16-word Vaishnava mantra which is mentioned in the Kali-Santarana Upanishad. *[Satyagraha](/wiki/Satyagraha_(opera) "Satyagraha (opera)")*, an opera by [Philip Glass](/wiki/Philip_Glass "Philip Glass"), uses texts from the *Bhagavad Gita*, sung in Sanskrit.[[404]](#cite_note-439)[[405]](#cite_note-440) In 1996, English [psychedelic rock](/wiki/Psychedelic_rock "Psychedelic rock") band [Kula Shaker](/wiki/Kula_Shaker "Kula Shaker") released *[Govinda](/wiki/Govinda_(Kula_Shaker_song) "Govinda (Kula Shaker song)")*, a song entirely sung in Sanskrit. The closing credits of *[The Matrix Revolutions](/wiki/The_Matrix_Revolutions "The Matrix Revolutions")* has a prayer from the *[Brihadaranyaka Upanishad](/wiki/Brihadaranyaka_Upanishad "Brihadaranyaka Upanishad")*. The song "Cyber-raga" from [Madonna](/wiki/Madonna "Madonna")'s album *[Music](/wiki/Music_(Madonna_album) "Music (Madonna album)")* includes Sanskrit chants,[[406]](#cite_note-Morgan_2011-441) and *Shanti/Ashtangi* from her 1998 album *[Ray of Light](/wiki/Ray_of_Light "Ray of Light")*, which won a Grammy, is the [ashtanga vinyasa yoga](/wiki/Ashtanga_vinyasa_yoga "Ashtanga vinyasa yoga") chant.[[407]](#cite_note-442) The lyrics include the mantra *Om shanti*.[[408]](#cite_note-443) Composer [John Williams](/wiki/John_Williams "John Williams") featured choirs singing in Sanskrit for *[Indiana Jones and the Temple of Doom](/wiki/Indiana_Jones_and_the_Temple_of_Doom "Indiana Jones and the Temple of Doom")* and in *[Star Wars: Episode I – The Phantom Menace](/wiki/Star_Wars:_Episode_I_%E2%80%93_The_Phantom_Menace "Star Wars: Episode I – The Phantom Menace")*.[[409]](#cite_note-444)[[410]](#cite_note-445)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] The theme song of *[Battlestar Galactica 2004](/wiki/Battlestar_Galactica_(2004_TV_series) "Battlestar Galactica (2004 TV series)")* is the [Gayatri Mantra](/wiki/Gayatri_Mantra "Gayatri Mantra"), taken from the [Rigveda](/wiki/Rigveda "Rigveda").[[411]](#cite_note-446) The lyrics of "The Child in Us" by [Enigma](/wiki/Enigma_(German_band) "Enigma (German band)") also contain Sanskrit verses.[[412]](#cite_note-447)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] In 2006, Mexican singer [Paulina Rubio](/wiki/Paulina_Rubio "Paulina Rubio") was influenced in Sanskrit for her concept album *[Ananda](/wiki/Ananda_(album) "Ananda (album)")*.[[413]](#cite_note-448)

11. **[^](#cite_ref-118)** Pāṇini's use of the term *lipi* has been a source of scholarly disagreements. Harry Falk in his 1993 overview states that ancient Indians neither knew nor used writing script, and Pāṇini's mention is likely a reference to Semitic and Greek scripts.[[102]](#cite_note-112) In his 1995 review, Salomon questions Falk's arguments and writes it is "speculative at best and hardly constitutes firm grounds for a late date for Kharoṣṭhī. The stronger argument for this position is that we have no specimen of the script before the time of Ashoka, nor any direct evidence of intermediate stages in its development; but of course this does not mean that such earlier forms did not exist, only that, if they did exist, they have not survived, presumably because they were not employed for monumental purposes before Ashoka".[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalomon1995271–279-113) According to Hartmut Scharfe, *lipi* of Pāṇini may be borrowed from the Old Persian *dipi*, in turn derived from Sumerian *dup*. Scharfe adds that the best evidence, at the time of his review, is that no script was used in India, aside from the Northwest Indian subcontinent, before c. 300 BCE because Indian tradition "at every occasion stresses the orality of the cultural and literary heritage".[[104]](#cite_note-Scharfe_2002-114) Kenneth Norman states writing scripts in ancient India evolved over the long period of time like other cultures, that it is unlikely that ancient Indians developed a single complete writing system at one and the same time in the Maurya era. It is even less likely, states Norman, that a writing script was invented during Ashoka's rule, starting from nothing, for the specific purpose of writing his inscriptions and then it was understood all over South Asia where the Ashoka pillars are found.[[105]](#cite_note-Hinüber1989-115) Goody (1987) states that ancient India likely had a "very old culture of writing" along with its oral tradition of composing and transmitting knowledge, because the Vedic literature is too vast, consistent and complex to have been entirely created, memorized, accurately preserved and spread without a written system.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoody1987110–124-116) Falk disagrees with Goody, and suggests that it is a Western presumption and inability to imagine that remarkably early scientific achievements such as Pāṇini's grammar (5th to 4th century BCE), and the creation, preservation and wide distribution of the large corpus of the Brahmanic Vedic literature and the Buddhist canonical literature, without any writing scripts. Bronkhorst (2002) disagrees with Falk, and states, "Falk goes too far. It is fair to expect that we believe that Vedic memorisation—though without parallel in any other human society—has been able to preserve very long texts for many centuries without losing a syllable. ... However, the oral composition of a work as complex as Pāṇini's grammar is not only without parallel in other human cultures, it is without parallel in India itself. ... It just will not do to state that our difficulty in conceiving any such thing is our problem".[[107]](#cite_note-bronkhorst2002lar-117)

"According to [Asko Parpola](/wiki/Asko_Parpola "Asko Parpola"), the Proto-Indo-Aryan civilization was influenced by two external waves of migrations. The first group originated from the southern Urals (c. 2100 BCE) and mixed with the peoples of the Bactria-Margiana Archaeological Complex (BMAC); this group then proceeded to South Asia, arriving around 1900 BCE. The second wave arrived in northern South Asia around 1750 BCE and mixed with the formerly arrived group, producing the Mitanni Aryans (c. 1500 BCE), a precursor to the peoples of the *Ṛgveda*. [Michael Witzel](/wiki/Michael_Witzel "Michael Witzel") has assigned an approximate chronology to the strata of Vedic languages, arguing that the language of the *Ṛgveda* changed through the beginning of the Iron Age in South Asia, which started in the Northwest (Punjab) around 1000 BCE. On the basis of comparative philological evidence, Witzel has suggested a five-stage periodization of Vedic civilization, beginning with the *Ṛgveda*. On the basis of internal evidence, the *Ṛgveda* is dated as a late Bronze Age text composed by pastoral migrants with limited settlements, probably between 1350 and 1150 BCE in the Punjab region."

"The story of the growth of arithmetic from the ancient inheritance to the wealth passed on to the Renaissance is dramatic and passes through several cultures. The most groundbreaking achievement was the evolution of a positional number system, in which the position of a digit within a number determines its value according to powers (usually) of ten (e.g., in 3,285, the "2" refers to hundreds). Its extension to include decimal fractions and the procedures that were made possible by its adoption transformed the abilities of all who calculated, with an effect comparable to the modern invention of the electronic computer. Roughly speaking, this began in India, was transmitted to Islam, and then to the Latin West."

"The importance of the Rigveda for the study of early Indo-Aryan historical linguistics cannot be underestimated. ... its language is ... notably similar in many respects to the most archaic poetic texts of related language families, the Old Avestan Gathas and Homer's Iliad and Odyssey, respectively the earliest poetic representatives of the Iranian and Greek language families. Moreover, its manner of preservation, by a system of oral transmission which has preserved the hymns almost without change for 3,000 years, makes it a very trustworthy witness to the Indo-Aryan language of North India in the second millennium BC. Its importance for the reconstruction of Proto-Indo-European, particularly in respect of the archaic morphology and syntax it preserves, ... is considerable. Any linguistic investigation into Old Indo-Aryan, Indo-Iranian, or Proto-Indo-European cannot avoid treating the evidence of the Rigveda as of vital importance."

223. **[^](#cite_ref-240)** This *visarga* is a consonant, not a vowel. It is a post-vocalic [voiceless glottal fricative](/wiki/Voiceless_glottal_fricative "Voiceless glottal fricative") [h], and an [allophone](/wiki/Allophone "Allophone") of *s* (or less commonly *r*) usually in word-final position. Some traditions of recitation append an echo of the preceding vowel after the [h] (Wikner, Charles (1996). ["A Practical Sanskrit Introductory"](http://sanskritdocuments.org/learning_tutorial_wikner/index.html). p. 6. [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20080618194654/http://sanskritdocuments.org/learning_tutorial_wikner/index.html) from the original on 18 June 2008. Retrieved 26 June 2020.): इः [ihi]. [Masica 1993](#CITEREFMasica1993), p. 146 considers the *visarga*, along with the letters ङ *ṅa* and ञ *ña* for the "largely predictable" [velar](/wiki/Velar_nasal "Velar nasal") and [palatal nasals](/wiki/Palatal_nasal "Palatal nasal"), to be examples of "phonetic overkill in the [writing] system".
